Back to main page| How a sail (and wing) worksA wing deflects air down. As the air is pushed down, the wing is pushed up. The force that pushes the air down is the same force that pushes the wing up. Compare to: When you are standing on the floor there is a force supporting you, and a force pushing the floor down. This is the same force. Now the question remains, why the air is deflected. At the lower side of the wing (=windward side of a sail) the air is deflected in a very simple way: Going straight is not possible because the wing is blocking this option. The leeward of the sail (=upper part of a wing) is also changing the direction of the air passing it. This is due too the Coanda effect: Air tends to follow a curved surface as long as the curvature is not too much. Something as sketched down here is happening. A wing is deflecting air with the bottom as well as the top of the wing. The air being deflected at the lower side of the wing is for most people easy to understand. The air being also deflected by the upper side of the wing, by the Coanda effect, is harder to understand. Later I continue about the Coanda effect, first something about what this theory results in. One could imagine from the theory above that a flat plate works as well as a curved profile. Unfortunately this is not completely true. That is because the air going up the wing (leeward of the sail) has to make a very tight turn around the sharp leading edge when under an angle. If this turn is too sharp the air can not follow the upper surface of the wing (the leeward side of the sail), and will be deflected less. In aviation flow separation of a wing is called "stall". Curvature makes this turn less sharp. Beside the sharpness of the turn also the amount of not moving air at leeward matters. If the wing (or sail) has a rough surface it tends to slow down the air by friction, resulting in not moving air staying at leeward. This explains why sail are never rough like a carpet. Again, a sail works by deflecting the air. This deflection results in a force -The sailforce- working perpendicular to the sail. That this force is perpendicular to the sail is important to understand and apply couple and forces Now in more detail the Coanda effect: In the right picture one can see the start situation. From the dotted area the air is being "washed" away by the friction of tf the fastmoving air. This is viscosity. De dotted air is disappearing. This would imply that there would be no air at all and that there would be a very low pressure. The air from the fast-moving air is thus being sucked to this area, and thereby deflects the air. So why does the flow separate from the curved surface sometimes? That is due to the friction between air and the surface. This friction slows down the air, and "refills" the dotted area with not moving air. This area then sucks less and if the fast-moving air is then deflected too much it can not be sucked completely to the surface. The air close the surface that has been slowed down is called boundary layer. This boundary layer grows when moving to the trailing edge. This implies that there can be more curvature in the front(=luff) of the wing as there can be at the aft part of the wing(=leech). A flow follows a curve because friction between air (=viscosity) and separates if there is too much friction between the air and the surface. Some simple experiments to show this can be found here Using this theory.When one wants as much force as possible directed forward one has to deflect as much as possible air aft. If one can achieve something as down here one is doing it right. Often confused with this too much deflecting to the windward side: Also one has to watch out for stall. In above stalling sail the air has to make a tight turn at the leading edge. This can be too much. If one can give a little bit more curve in the forward part, the air moves around smoother, and will not easy separate. Off course one also has to take care to sheet in the sail enough, else the forward part will not do anything. When having less curvature one needs to pull in the sheet less to have the luff doing anything. A flat sail can be used to improve pointing, but is gives less force. above: when one would go more to the wind the luff would start flapping. If then one sheets in the sail more it would be deflecting air to the windward side. Until now we only discussed curvature in length direction, but a sail often also has curvature in height direction: Twist. The upper part of the sail is more pointing outward then close to the bottom. A little twist is good, since the higher one gets the harder the wind gets, and thus the apparent wind changes more to abeam. Een beetje twist is gunstig, aangezien hoe hoger je komt hoe harder de wind, en dus hoe ruimer de wind inkomt. Interesting is that with some heeling and twist one can make the sail more flat, from the winds point of view. The blue line represents the path the air is going along the sail. In the right picture the air is only deflected a little. without twist the path of air is not much changed: Because one can sail more upwind with a flat sail one can see that with some twist and heeling one can sail more up wind. Unfortunately this will reduce the sailforce. Nice theory, but how can you see how and if the flow is following your sail? I recommend telltales and gentry tales, more about them in tuning but before you start there I recommend to first read couples and Forces The theory of deflecting as much wind as possible aft has an error. When one is sailing close to the wind and the trailing edge of the sail is directed aft one is doing it right. However, this does not mean when one is sailing before the wind one should have the sail sheeted in almost completely. In that case the sail is not working as a wing but as a parachute, slowing as much air as possible. couples and forces As explained above deflecting the wind results in the sailforce. This force is given to the sail by pressures, A lower pressure at the leeward side and a higher pressure at the windward side. Air has the tendency to be sucked to the lower pressure area from the higher pressure area. The air in front of the sail is also sucked to the lower pressure area on the leeward side. This means that there will be more air flowing on the leeward side as on the windward side. This results in that deflection on the leeward side is more important as on the windward side, because there is more air to deflect. The ropes in the sail show that the speed is much higher on the leeward side. Some people say that the air is speeding up because of the Bernoulli effect. They say the lower pressure is created by the higher speed. They are right, but it does not help understanding the flow around a sail to my opinion. Also the Law of Bernoulli is applied wrong because it is not a very simple law. My advice is not to use Bernoulli law to explain sailing. For people who want to know more about Bernoullis law please click here. Tipvortexes.(Also only for the real theory cracks) Beside the air "leaking" from the higher windward pressure around the mast to the leeward part as explained above air is also leaking around the lower side of the sail. This leaking is a deflection to the wrong side and is not helping. The force that results from this deflection is in the wrong direction. It results in less driving force and more sideway force. See the picture below (I should have drawn only the red force, or only the orange force, and not all together) (For airplanes the increase in sideway force is called "induced drag", and the loss in driving force "loss of lift") To reduce this leaking one needs to have a sail with only a small area where this leaking occurs, so a real high sail with a short length. In general such a sail is called a "high Aspect sail" This leaking of air can be easily detected by holding a windex just below your sail. It will point to the higher pressure level -the windward side- much more as the windex on top of your mast. This leaking also explains the needed twist: If one would hold a very small sail just below the sail it should be sheeted in much less as the mainsail, because it is in the "leaking" air. same goes for the upper end of your sail, what is actually being much smaller because the sail is tapered. Sometimes this is called "taper induced twist" The leaking air in front of the sail explains why the jib is sheeted in less as the main. The jib is in the leaking air of the mainsail. Of course the main is also in the pre-deflected air of the jib, making it also necessary to sheet in the main more as the jib. Not the same I want the ones you say no to. Said the pig, and he rolled in it. Lesson 19 Section 2 This means that if one of the quantities doubles, then the other will become half as large. For the inverse of the ratio 2 to 1 ("doubles") is the ratio 1 to 2 ("half"). The terms are exchanged. Example 1. Let us suppose that the time it takes to do a job is inversely proportional to the number of workers. The more workers, the shorter the time. Specifically: If 6 workers can do a job in 4 days, then how long will it take 12 workers? Answer. The number of workers has doubled , going from 6 to 12. Therefore it will take only half as many days. It will take only 2 days. Example 2. The speed that a car can achieve in 10 seconds is inversely proportional to its weight. (That is, the more the car weighs, the slower it will be going.) After 10 seconds, a car that weighs 2400 pounds can achieve a speed of 44 miles per hour. If the car weighed 1600 pounds, how fast would it be going? Answer. What ratio has the new weight to the original weight -- 1600 pounds to 2400 pounds? 1600 is two thirds of 2400: 1600 is to 2400 as 16 is to 24 as 2 is to 3. (After ignoring the 0's, we see that both 16 and 24 have a common divisor 8. Lesson 16, Question 7.) Now, the inverse ratio of 2 to 3 is the ratio 3 to 2. And since 3 = 3 is one and a half times 2. The speed therefore will be one and a half times 44: 44 + 22 = 66 miles per hour. When quantities are inversely proportional, we say that one of them varies inversely as the other. Thus the speed that a car can achieve in a given time varies inversely as its weight. Please "turn" the page and do some Problems. Continue on to the next Lesson. Title: How to Do a Side Braid A side braid is a stylish and chic way to wear your hair, perfect for both casual and formal occasions. This hairstyle can be achieved with medium-length to long hair. Here's our comprehensive, step-by-step guide on creating a beautiful side braid. ### Step 1: Brush Your Hair Before starting any hairstyle, begin by gently brushing your hair using a paddle brush or a vented brush. This will help remove tangles and knots, making the braiding process smoother and easier. It also ensures that there are no lumps or bumps in the final look. ### Step 2: Choose Your Parting Decide where you want to part your hair. A deep side part adds drama and sophistication, while a subtle side part creates a more natural and relaxed appearance. Use the tail end of your comb to create a clean part line. ### Step 3: Gather Your Hair Gently gather all of your hair and bring it towards the shoulder opposite from the parting. Keep the hair smooth as you collect it; this will make the braid neater and more polished. If you have layers that frame your face, consider gathering them into the braid as well. ### Step 4: Divide the Hair into Three Sections After the death of her mother, Leslie Stephen was not able to keep good relations in the family. But the laser kept going around and around, just a little out of Elle's reach. Elle kept trying to catch the laser, clapping and shouting in excitement. She was very persistent and didn't give up until the laser finally let her catch it. Elle was delighted and proudly carried the laser home. Title: How to Fit More Pictures on a Scrapbook Page ================================================== Are you running out of space on your scrapbook pages but still want to include all those cherished memories? This tutorial will guide you through the process of fitting more pictures onto a single scrapbook page while maintaining an organized and aesthetically pleasing layout. By following these steps, you'll be able to create visually appealing scrapbook pages without sacrificing any precious moments. ### Step 1: Choose a Suitable Layout Before you start arranging photos, select a layout that allows for maximum picture placement. Consider using grids or templates designed specifically for multiple photo arrangements. These can be found online or created using graphic design software. A well-planned layout ensures that there is enough white space between images, preventing overcrowding and creating visual interest. Key tip: Keep in mind the orientation (portrait vs landscape) of your photographs when choosing a layout. You may need to adjust the template if most of your pictures are vertical or horizontal. ### Step 2: Resize Photos Properly Resizing photos is crucial when trying to fit more images on a page. However, it's essential to maintain the aspect ratio of each image to avoid distortion. Use editing tools like Adobe Photoshop or free alternatives such as GIMP or Canva to resize your photos proportionally. Guideline: Aim to fill at least 70% of the available space with photos, leaving room for embellishments and journaling. ### Step 3: Arrange Photos Strategically Begin placing larger photos first, then fill gaps with smaller ones. Alternate between portrait and landscape orientations to optimize space usage. Additionally, consider overlapping some images slightly to save space and add dimension to your layout. Key tip: Leave adequate negative space around each photo to ensure they don't appear cluttered and allow for easy viewing. ### Step 4: Utilize Borders and Mattes Borders and mattes can help separate photos and make them stand out individually. They also provide additional opportunities to incorporate colors and patterns from your theme. Opt for thin borders to maximize the visible area of your photos. Guideline: Stick to one or two border styles per page to maintain consistency and cohesion. ### Step 5: Add Journaling Cards or Tags Incorporating journaling cards or tags into your design provides extra spaces for notes, quotes, or other text elements without taking up too much real estate on your page. Plus, they offer another opportunity to enhance your theme with coordinating colors and patterns. Key tip: Place journaling cards near related photos to keep captions clear and concise. ### Step 6: Embellish Thoughtfully While adding decorative elements can bring life to your scrapbook page, be cautious not to overdo it. Limit yourself to a few key pieces that complement your photos and tie together your overall design. Remember, less is often more when it comes to embellishing. Guideline: Choose embellishments that serve a purpose, whether it's drawing attention to specific areas or enhancing the story told by your photos. By following these steps, you'll be able to fit more pictures on a scrapbook page without compromising aesthetic appeal. With practice, organizing and designing creative layouts will become second nature - giving you the freedom to preserve even more memories in your treasured albums. oh, I think he wants some sushi. Crafting Candles in Minecraft: A Comprehensive Guide =================================================== Minecraft is a popular sandbox game that allows players to build and explore virtual worlds made up of blocky 3D objects. One of the many activities you can do in Minecraft is crafting items using resources found within the game world. This guide will focus on teaching you how to craft candles in Minecraft. By following these steps, you'll be able to create your own decorative candles to add ambiance to your Minecraft structures! Step 1: Gather Resources ------------------------ To start crafting candles, you need three essential ingredients: string, honeycomb, and any type of fuel (such as coal or sticks). Here's where to find them: * **String**: You can obtain string by killing spiders, which drop 0-2 strings upon death. Alternatively, you can shear sheep that have been naturally spawned with grass on their backs to get wool, then craft 4 wool blocks into 3 strings through the crafting table interface. * **Honeycomb**: To collect honeycombs, you must first locate an active beehive or nest. These are usually found hanging from tree branches or inside caves. Once located, equip yourself with an empty bottle and interact with the hive or nest to gather honey. Afterward, place the honey bottles in a furnace along with fuel like wood or charcoal to turn the honey into honeycomb. * **Fuel**: Various materials can serve as fuel for candle creation, including coal, wooden logs, sticks, or even blaze rods if you happen to venture into the Nether dimension. Coal can be mined from rocks underground, while wooden logs come from chopping down trees. Sticks can be obtained either by breaking tall grass or by placing two wooden planks diagonally adjacent to one another in the crafting grid. Blaze rods are dropped by blazes, hostile mobs that reside in Nether Fortresses. Step 2: Open Your Crafting Table ------------------------------- Once you have gathered all necessary resources, open your crafting table to access its 3x3 crafting grid. The crafting table provides more space than your inventory's 2x2 grid and enables you to combine multiple items at once. ![Image description]( Blankets and a pillow and a carriage and a dolly? Title: How to Deal with a Boss who is Interested in You Introduction: Navigating workplace relationships can be tricky, especially when your boss shows interest in you beyond the professional realm. This article aims to provide guidance on handling such situations while maintaining a healthy work environment and respecting boundaries. Remember that every individual's experience is unique; therefore, adapt these steps as necessary to fit your circumstances. Step 1: Understand the Situation Recognize the signs indicating your boss might be interested in you. These could include prolonged eye contact, frequent one-on-one meetings, personal questions, compliments unrelated to work, or physical touch (within limits). Acknowledging the situation allows you to respond appropriately. Key Tip: Keep communication open but focused on work matters until you have clarified their intentions. Step 2: Analyze Your Feelings Determine how this attention makes you feel - comfortable, uncomfortable, flattered, threatened? Consider whether this interest affects your job performance or creates an awkward atmosphere at work. Knowing your emotions will help guide your actions moving forward. Guideline: Trust your instincts; if something feels off, it probably is. Step 3: Set Professional Boundaries Make it clear that while you appreciate the recognition, you would prefer to maintain a strictly professional relationship. Use phrases like "I enjoy working together professionally" or "Let's focus on our tasks." By setting boundaries early, you minimize potential misunderstandings. Key Tip: Be consistent with enforcing these boundaries to avoid mixed signals. Step 4: Document Everything Keep records of all interactions, emails, texts, etc., related to work. Should any issues arise, documented evidence supports your case. It also ensures there are no miscommunications about expectations or goals. Guideline: Overcommunicating via email or chat can serve as proof later if needed. Step 5: Seek Advice from HR or Mentors Discuss the situation confidentially with someone impartial, such as a mentor or Human Resources representative. They offer objective advice based on experience and company policies. Their insight may prove valuable in managing the situation effectively. Key Tip: Choose a trusted advisor who understands office dynamics and has your best interests at heart. Step 6: Remain Calm and Composed Maintain your cool even when feelings get hurt or tempers rise. Losing control could exacerbate the problem, leading to more complications down the line. Instead, remain calm and collected, addressing concerns rationally and diplomatically. Guideline: Practice deep breathing exercises before important conversations to stay composed. Step 7: Report Harassment If Necessary If the behavior continues despite attempts to set boundaries, consider reporting it to HR as harassment. Provide concrete examples, dates, times, and witnesses if available. Companies usually take employee complaints seriously and investigate thoroughly. Key Tip: Familiarize yourself with your organization's sexual harassment policy to understand what constitutes reportable behavior. Step 8: Prioritize Self-Care Workplace romances, regardless of hierarchy, can cause stress. Make sure to prioritize self-care activities outside of work hours. Exercise regularly, eat healthily, get enough sleep, and engage in hobbies or social events that bring joy and relaxation. Guideline: Surrounding oneself with supportive friends and family during challenging times provides emotional stability. Conclusion: Managing a boss's interest requires finesse and assertiveness. Following these steps enables you to handle the situation gracefully while protecting both your professional reputation and mental wellbeing. [the soldier's jacket is composed of three pieces] [clears all the silver men off the black base] Title: How to Build Shoe Insoles Building your own shoe insoles can be a cost-effective way to get customized support and cushioning for your feet. This tutorial will guide you through the process, from measuring your feet to creating a comfortable and durable insole. **Things You'll Need:** * Cardboard or foam board * Pencil * Ruler or measuring tape * Scissors or craft knife * Glue gun and glue sticks * EVA (Ethylene Vinyl Acetate) foam sheets (available at most craft stores) * Fabric (optional) **Step 1: Measure Your Feet** To ensure a proper fit, start by tracing the outline of both of your feet onto cardboard or foam board using a pencil. Stand with your weight evenly distributed on both feet while doing this. Be sure to include space around the perimeter of your foot for added comfort. Use a ruler or measuring tape to record the length and width of each foot. **Key Tip**: If there is more than a half inch difference between your two feet, build the larger size insole. It is better for an insole to be slightly too big rather than too small. **Step 2: Create a Template** Using the measurements taken in Step 1, draw a rectangle around each traced foot that matches those dimensions. Cut out these rectangles carefully with scissors or a craft knife, ensuring straight lines. These cutouts are now templates for shaping your EVA foam insoles. **Step 3: Trace Outlines Onto Foam Sheets** Place one template on top of an EVA foam sheet, aligning the edges as closely as possible. Using the template as a guide, trace the outline lightly with a pencil. Repeat this step until all necessary parts are accounted for - typically, you need two main pieces corresponding to the soles of your shoes plus additional sections if extra arch support or heel cups are required. **Step 4: Cut Out Foam Sections** Carefully cut along the marked lines to create individual foam sections matching your templates. Try stacking multiple layers together if thicker cushioning is needed. **Step 5: Arrange Layers for Optimal Support** Determine where arch supports and heel cups should go based on personal preference and any existing foot conditions (consultation with medical professionals may be beneficial here). Stack and arrange the appropriate foam layers accordingly. **Step 6: Secure Layers Together** Heat up your glue gun and apply hot glue sparingly along the seams where different foam layers meet. Press them firmly together until secure. Allow time for the glue to cool completely before moving forward. **Step 7: Add Finishing Touches** For aesthetic appeal, cover the entire surface of the finished insole(s) with fabric. Simply wrap the material tightly around the insole, securing it with hot glue every few inches. Trim off excess fabric after completion. **Guidelines for Success:** * Always remember safety first when working with sharp objects like scissors and knives. * Double check measurements before cutting; accuracy matters! * Test out prototypes before finalizing design decisions. * Keep user manual handy while operating electronic devices such as a glue gun. * Clean workspace regularly during projects for efficiency and precision. By following these steps meticulously, you'll end up with a pair of DIY shoe insoles tailored perfectly to your unique foot shape and needs. Happy building! Title: Draw Animals in Detailed Steps Introduction: Drawing animals can be a fun and rewarding experience. It allows you to capture their beauty and express your creativity. This tutorial will guide you through the process of drawing various animals in simple steps. We'll start with basic shapes and gradually add details to create lifelike drawings. Section 1: Understanding Basic Shapes Every animal can be broken down into simpler geometric forms like circles, ovals, squares, triangles etc. Identifying these shapes helps establish proportions and perspective before adding intricate features. Step 1a: Outline Basic Structure Sketch light lines to form the body structure using ovals for the chest and hips, cylinders for legs, and spheres for heads. For example, when drawing a dog, make the back oval larger than the front one, representing its sloping back. Key Tip: Keep initial sketches light so they can be erased or adjusted without affecting final details. Guideline: Practice identifying and sketching basic shapes in different animals until you feel comfortable. Step 1b: Add Guiding Lines Add construction lines within the outlined shape to indicate joints, spine, and facial features. These help ensure accurate placement of limbs and head. Key Tip: Remember that animals' skeletal structures differ; research specific species for accuracy. Guideline: Use reference photos if needed, especially when starting out. Section 2: Building Detail Now that we have our guideline, we begin building up more recognizable features. Step 2a: Refine Body Features Turn vague shapes into distinctive parts - muzzle, paws, tail, etc., still keeping them rough. Key Tip: Focus on overall contours rather than small crevices at this stage. ' Nestle to my heart! Now give me a sigh!' In the United States, people say the seasons begin at the solstices and equinoxes. The summer solstice is the longest day of the year, and the winter solstice is the shortest. The equinox is the time when the day and the night are the same number of hours, assuming the sun were a point of light at its center. Since civil dawn occurs when the edge of the sun first appears over the horizon and civil dusk occurs when the edge drops over the horizon, the civil length of day is 12 hours long several days prior to the equinoxes. In the USA, summer begins at summer solstice, winter at winter solstice, spring at the spring ("vernal") equinox and autumn at the autumnal equinox. You don't put your mouth on it though. Today we're going to learn about using a special online tool called zbMATH. This tool helps us find information about different math concepts and topics. It's like having your own personal librarian who knows everything there is to know about math! Let me show you some cool features of zbMATH. First, did you know that you can search for things using just one word, like "Geometry"? That's right! Just type it into the search bar, and zbMATH will give you lots of information on geometry. And don't worry about capitalizing letters - zbMATH doesn't care whether you write "geometry," "Geometry," or even "GEOMETRY." Now, let's say you want to learn more about something specific within geometry, like topological groups. You can do this by putting the phrase inside quotes, like "'topological group'". This tells zbMATH to look for those words together, instead of separately. Another neat feature is wildcards (*). Imagine you want to explore various ideas around functions but don't remember their names exactly. No problem! Typing "funct*" brings up anything starting with "funct", like "functions" or "functorial". Pretty cool, huh? But wait, there's more! What if you'd like to discover authors or titles connected to certain subjects? Easy peasy! Simply enter "au: Author Name & ti: Title Keyword(s)" - replace "Author Name" with the actual name of the person you're looking for, and put keywords from the title between the quotation marks. Voila! Results appear before your eyes. And guess what? You can also narrow down your searches based on when the material was published or where it came from. By adding "py:" followed by a year, you get info from that specific year. Or try entering "so:" along with a shortened version of a journal's name (for example, "Eur* J* Mat* Soc*") to see content from that source. There are many other ways to use zbMATH, but these basics will get you started on exploring the world of mathematics in new and exciting ways. So go ahead, dive in, and happy learning! I stubbed by toe Rationale: When learning to read and spell words, children need to understand vowel digraphs so they can match letters to phonemes. In this lesson, children will learn the ou=/ow/ correspondence. They will learn to recognize it in both spoken and written words by practicing reading and spelling in this lesson. A letterbox lesson will be implemented to help children understand the ou = /ow/ correspondence. Materials: Elkonin Letterboxes for each and letters for each student: c, d, g, h, ch, l, m, n, o, r, s, t, u, chart paper with tongue twister, flash cards with letterbox words on them, The Napping House by Audrey Wood, a box of band aids, picture worksheet Procedures: 1. Introduce the lesson by writing the "ou" on the board. When we see the o and u together, they make the /ou/ sound in many words. Today we are going to practice recognizing ou as /ow/ in spoken and written words. 2. Ask students: Have you ever stumped you toe on something and said OW!? Well, when o and u are together in words, they team up to say /ow/. Let's all say that together "ow!" 3. Write, "I found out about her loud mouth" on chart paper. Read tongue twister to the students and then say Now let's all say it together 'I found out about her loud mouth.' Let's say it again, this time stretching out the /ow/ in each word: I f oow nd oow-t ab oow t her l oow d m oow th. Great job everyone! 4. Ask students to take out their Letterboxes and envelope of letters. Now we are going to spell out some letters with ou in them. When we spell the words, we will spell only one sound in each box. Because ou works as a team to spell /ow/, both o and u will go in one box. Are there any questions? Model an example on the board for students and ask them to follow along with you at their desk. (ex word: about) Words for students to spell: Out, loud, mouth, couch, stout, sound, ground (Shows number of boxes needed) Ou-t, l-ou-d, m-ou-th, c-ou-ch, s-t-ou-t, s-ou-n-d, g-r-ou-n-d Say each word slowly, emphasizing the /ow/ and use each word with a sentence. Give students time to spell each word and walk around the room throughout the activity to see how students are doing. 5. Allow students to choose a partner and give each pair of students a set of flash cards with letterbox words on them. Now we are going to practice reading the words we have just spelled. Show the cards to your partner one at a time to practice reading the words. When they have finished the two of you will switch the cards and the other partner will read the words. 6. Distribute one band aid to each student. Read The Napping House aloud to students. Ask them to hold up their band-aids whenever they hear a word with /ow/ in it. Leave the book in the reading center for students to practice reading on their own or obtain multiple copies of the book for individual practice. 7. For assessment, distribute worksheets to students. Tell them to circle the pictures of the words containing the /ow/ phonemes and then write the spelling of the word beneath the picture. (Pictures included: book, couch, house, lamp, mouse, stove, window, ground) Eldredge, J. Lloyd. (1995) Teaching Decoding in Holistic Classrooms. New Jersey: Prentice All, 105-018 Buck, Lauren. In chemistry, a crucible is a kind of laboratory equipment that is usually a small cup, about the size of a shot glass, made of porcelain or non-reactive metal. Crucibles are used to heat chemical compounds to very high temperatures using a gas burner. One of the earliest uses of platinum was as a material in crucibles. Title: How to Perform a Bunny Hop on a Bicycle Introduction: Bunny hopping is an impressive bike trick that involves jumping over obstacles while keeping your momentum going. It's a useful skill to learn if you want to navigate rough terrains or simply show off your biking skills. This tutorial will guide you through the process of performing a bunny hop on a bicycle, breaking down each movement into simple steps. Remember, practice makes perfect; don't be discouraged if you don't get it right away. **Safety Precautions:** Before attempting any tricks on your bicycle, ensure that you are wearing appropriate safety gear such as a helmet, gloves, and knee pads. Practice in a safe area free from traffic and other hazards. If possible, have someone experienced spot you during your practice sessions. Step 1: Prepare Your Bike Make sure your bike is in good working condition. Adjust the seat height so that both feet can touch the ground when seated but still allow for enough leg extension when standing over the frame. Proper tire pressure also contributes to better control and stability. Step 2: Basic Balance Check Stand next to your bike with one foot on the pedal and the other on the ground. Push off gently and start riding slowly. Try lifting your back wheel by pushing down on the pedals with your dominant foot (the foot corresponding to your strong hand) and simultaneously pulling up the handlebars. Repeat this motion several times until you feel comfortable raising the rear wheel without losing balance. Step 3: Front Wheel Lift Once you've mastered lifting the back wheel, try lifting the front wheel. While moving forward at low speed, crouch slightly and then quickly straighten your legs and pull upwards on the handlebars. The idea here is to transfer weight from the wheels to the frame, causing the front wheel to leave the ground. Keep practicing until you can consistently lift the front wheel smoothly. Step 4: Combining Both Motions Now that you can independently lift both wheels, it's time to combine these movements. Ride slowly, then crouch and prepare to jump. As you spring up, push down on the pedal of your dominant foot, pull up on the handlebar, and thrust your hips forward. The goal is to raise both wheels off the ground simultaneously. Land softly, absorbing the impact with bent knees. Step 5: Adding Height & Distance To increase the height and distance covered during a bunny hop, focus on exploding upward rather than just lifting the wheels. Use your arms and legs forcefully to propel yourself higher. Visualize leaping over an object instead of merely hopping. Key Tips & Guidelines: - **Patience**: Mastering the bunny hop takes time and patience. Don't rush the learning process - take breaks whenever needed and always prioritize safety. - **Balance**: Maintaining balance is crucial. Developing good balance starts with being able to ride comfortably at slow speeds. - **Strength**: Building strength in your legs and core will help improve your bunny hops. Regular exercises like squats and planks can enhance your performance. - **Fearlessness**: Overcoming fear is essential. Start small and gradually build confidence. Never attempt dangerous stunts beyond your skill level. Conclusion: Performing a successful bunny hop requires coordination, balance, and strength. By following this comprehensive guide and dedicating sufficient practice time, you'll soon be impressively hopping over obstacles on your bike! According to a Canadian survey of "Now" magazine readers, (cited above), the frequency of masturbation declines after the age of 17. However, most males masturbate daily, or even more frequently, well into their 20s and sometimes far beyond. This decline is more drastic among females, and more gradual among males. While females aged 13-17 masturbated almost once a day on average (and almost as often as their male peers), adult women only masturbated 8-9 times a month, compared to the 18-22 among men. It is also apparent that masturbation frequency declines with age. So, let me try my best to simplify this concept so that it's easy enough for grade-schoolers to understand. Imagine you have a piggy bank full of coins of different values - say pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters. You want to know how many different ways there are to make change for a dollar using these coins. This is called the "Coin Change Problem." One way to solve this problem is by using something called "Dynamic Programming," which is just a fancy name for breaking down a big problem into smaller subproblems that we can solve more easily. In this case, we can break down the problem based on the remaining amount we need to make up $1, and also based on which type of coin we are currently considering using. To keep track of all the possible solutions we come up with along the way, we create a table (or matrix), where each row represents the remaining amount we still need to make up, and each column represents the type of coin we are currently considering. Then, for each cell in the table, we store the number of ways to make change for that particular combination of remaining amount and coin type. For example, suppose we are currently considering making change using dimes. The first row of the table would represent the case when we don't need any more coins to reach $1 (i.e., we already have exactly $1). The second row would represent the case when we need one more cent to reach $1 (i.e., we have 99 cents left). And so on. Similarly, the first column would correspond to the case when we are not allowed to use any more dimes (i.e., we've already used up all the available dimes). The second column would correspond to the case when we are allowed to use exactly one more dime, and so on. Now, here comes the interesting part. To fill in each cell in the table, we consider two possibilities: either we include the current coin type in our solution, or we exclude it. If we choose to include the current coin type, then we add its value to the previous row (corresponding to the reduced remaining amount), and copy over the result from that cell. On the other hand, if we choose to exclude the current coin type, then we simply move on to the next column without adding anything to the previous row. By doing this recursively for each cell in the table, we eventually arrive at the bottom-right corner of the table, which corresponds to the total number of ways to make change for $1 using all four types of coins. The reason why we need extra memory to solve this problem is because we need to store intermediate results in the table as we go along. Specifically, we need to store enough information to reconstruct the final answer once we get to the end of the table. However, since the table has dimensions N x M (where N is the maximum number of coins needed to make change for $1, and M is the number of coin types), the total amount of memory required is proportional to the product of N and M, rather than their sum. Therefore, the space complexity of this algorithm is O(NM), rather than O(M+N), as some people mistakenly believe. In summary, solving the Coin Change Problem requires us to build a table of intermediate results, where each entry represents the number of ways to make change for a given remaining amount and a given coin type. The space complexity of this algorithm is O(NM), where N is the maximum number of coins needed to make change for $1, and M is the number of coin types. This looks like there's a real mustard in it. Run around the playground a third time and run over to the brick house. Try huffing and puffing and try to blow the brick house down. The hay and sticks will likely blow over or fall since they are lighter than the bricks. The bricks should be heavy enough to not fall over as easily. Students will love acting out this story and will be able to recall the events and characters easily. [MOT is making noise clearing away the dishes] Getting a personalized license plate in Pennsylvania can be an exciting way to customize your vehicle and showcase your personality. This guide will walk you through the process, providing detailed steps and helpful tips along the way. ### Part 1: Researching Your Personalized Plate Options Step 1: Familiarize yourself with Pennsylvania's personalized license plate options. Pennsylvania offers various types of personalized plates, including standard, special organization, military, and collegiate plates. Each type has its own set of requirements and fees. Visit the Pennsylvania Department of Transportation (PennDOT) website to explore these options thoroughly. Key tip: Make sure to check if your preferred plate configuration is available before proceeding to the next steps. The PennDOT website provides an online search tool where you can verify availability. Step 2: Consider character limits and restrictions. Standard passenger personalized plates consist of six characters - letters or numbers, or a combination of both. Some specialty plates have different character limitations; consult the PennDOT website for specific details. Additionally, certain words, phrases, or combinations are prohibited due to obscenities, vulgarity, or confusion with official government identifiers. Guideline: Choose a unique phrase that represents you while adhering to all rules and regulations. Avoid using profanity, offensive language, or references to illegal activities. Part 2: Applying for Your Personalized Plate Step 3: Gather necessary documents and information. To apply for a personalized plate, you must provide proof of identity, such as a driver's license or ID card, and current registration for the vehicle(s) involved. If ordering by mail, include payment for the required fee, which varies depending on the type of plate chosen. Key tip: Keep copies of all submitted documentation and correspondence related to your application. It may help expedite any potential issues during processing. Step 4: Submit your application. You can submit your personalized plate application either online, via mail, or in person at a local PennDOT office. When applying online, create an account with PennDOT's Driver & Vehicle Services portal first. For mailed applications, send them to the following address: Bureau of Motor Vehicles Attn: Custom Plates Section P.O. Box 68597 Harrisburg, PA 17106-8597 When visiting a PennDOT office, bring all required documents and expect longer wait times due to higher demand. Step 5: Wait for approval. The review process typically takes four to six weeks from submission date. You will receive notification once your request is approved or denied. If approved, your new personalized plates and validation stickers will arrive separately within two to three weeks after notification. Key tip: Be patient throughout this process, as it may take several weeks before receiving your new plates. Continue using your old plates until the new ones arrive. Part 3: Installing and Displaying Your New Plates Step 6: Install your new plates properly. Once received, securely attach the front and rear plates to your vehicle according to state law. Failure to display valid plates can result in fines or other penalties. Step 7: Register your plates annually. Remember that personalized plates require annual renewal just like regular plates. Renewals can be completed online, by mail, or in person at a PennDOT office. Validation stickers should always be displayed on the upper right corner of the rear plate. By following these steps carefully, you can successfully obtain and enjoy a personalized license plate in Pennsylvania that reflects your individuality. Can the car go down the ramp? Creating a pie chart using percentages is an effective way to visually represent categorical data and show proportions or distributions. This guide will walk you through the process step-by-step using a spreadsheet program like Microsoft Excel or Google Sheets. **Step 1: Gather Your Data** Before creating your pie chart, collect all relevant data that needs representation. Make sure this data can be broken down into categories with distinct values. For instance, if you want to create a pie chart showing the distribution of grades in a class (A, B, C, D, F), gather the count of students who received each grade. **Step 2: Organize Your Data in a Spreadsheet** Enter your data into a spreadsheet so that each category has its own column and there's another column indicating their respective counts or quantities. If necessary, add additional rows for subcategories within main categories but ensure they share the same total quantity as their parent category. Here's an example table format: | Category | Count | Percentage | |---|---|---| | A | 35 | | | B | 28 | | | C | 20 | | | D | 12 | | | F | 5 | | **Step 3: Calculate the Total Sum of Quantities** Calculate the sum of all quantities under the 'Count' column. You may simply enter =SUM(B2:B6) into cell B7 if using Excel or Google Sheets. The result should appear as "100" since we are aiming for percentage later. **Step 4: Compute Individual Percentages** In the '%' column next to each number, calculate what percentage each value represents out of the total sum calculated earlier. Use the formula `=(value/total)*100`, replacing 'value' with the actual count and 'total' with the sum from Step 3. Apply this formula across every row in the '%' column. Continuing our previous example, cell C2 would contain `=(B2/$B$7)*100`. After filling out formulas for cells C2 through C6, format these numbers as percentages by selecting Format > Number > Percent in most applications. Your completed table should look similar to this: | Category | Count | Percentage | |---|---|---| | A | 35 | 35% | | B | 28 | 28% | | C | 20 | 20% | | D | 12 | 12% | | F | 5 | 5% | **Step 5: Create the Pie Chart** Highlight the entire range of your organized data including headers. Then go to Insert > Chart (or equivalent option based on your software). Choose 'Pie Chart' as the type of graph. Customize any visual aspects such as color scheme or label font size according to preference. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Always ensure that the data used adds up to 100%. If not, recheck calculations. - Keep labels clear and concise for easy understanding. - Consider sorting categories either alphabetically or numerically depending on context for better readability. - When dealing with many small slices, consider grouping them together into an "Other" category to maintain simplicity. - Be mindful of chart sizing; make sure text remains legible when viewing. In organisms which use chromosomal crossovers to exchange DNA and recombine genes, errors in alignment during meiosis can also cause mutations. Errors in crossover are especially likely when similar sequences cause partner chromosomes to adopt a mistaken alignment; this makes some regions in genomes more prone to mutating in this way. These errors create large structural changes in DNA sequence-duplications, inversions or deletions of entire regions, or the accidental exchanging of whole parts between different chromosomes (called translocation). Title: How to Create an Ombre Lip Art Look Introduction: Ombre lip art is a popular makeup trend that involves gradually blending two or more shades of lip color from light to dark, creating a gradient effect. This technique can be intimidating for beginners, but with patience and practice, you can master the look. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you create your own ombre lips like a pro. What You Need: * A concealer or foundation close to your skin tone * Lip liner (optional) * Three lipsticks or liquid lip colors, one lighter than your natural lip shade, one medium toned, and one darker * Small detail brushes or lip brush * Tissue paper or cotton swabs for corrections Step 1: Prep Your Lips Before starting the ombre process, make sure your lips are exfoliated and moisturized. Dry or chapped lips may result in uneven application and patchy areas. Apply a thin layer of balm and gently rub off any dead skin cells using a soft toothbrush. Remove excess product before proceeding. Step 2: Conceal and Line (Optional) Use concealer or foundation close to your skin tone to cover up your natural lip color. Applying this base will give you a clean canvas and ensure better adhesion of the lip products. If preferred, line your lips with a lipliner matching the lightest shade used. Outlining provides definition and prevents feathering. Step 3: Start With The Light Shade Begin by applying the lightest lipstick or liquid lip color all over your upper and lower lips using a small detail brush or lip brush. Make sure the edges blend well into the concealed area without leaving harsh lines. Keep building until you reach full opacity. Key Tip: Use minimal pressure while applying; pressing too hard might disrupt the evenness of the base coat. Step 4: Blend Into Medium Shade Next, pick up some of the medium-toned lip product onto your detail brush. Focusing only on the center part of both lips, carefully pat and blend outward towards the edge where the lightest hue fades away. Remember to maintain gradual transitions between the shades. Guideline: Maintain balance when working on either side of your mouth to avoid asymmetry. Step 5: Add Dark Accents Now, take the darkest lip color and dab it right at the middle of your bottom lip and top cupid's bow. Gently diffuse these spots into the surrounding medium tone ensuring seamless integration. Be cautious about overloading the dark hue as it could dominate the overall appearance if applied excessively. Step 6: Refine and Correct Check for uniformity across your lips. Any harsh borders should be smoothed out gently. Utilize tissue paper or cotton swabs to rectify mistakes promptly. Don't hesitate to reapply layers of previous shades if necessary. Step 7: Set Your Masterpiece Once satisfied with the final output, let the layered lipsticks settle down for few minutes to prevent bleeding or transferring. Afterwards, seal everything in place with setting powder or translucent loose pigment dusted lightly via a fluffy eyeshadow brush. Conclusion: Practice makes perfect - don't get discouraged if your first attempts aren't flawless. Follow these steps diligently, experiment with different color combinations, and soon enough, executing stunning ombre lip arts would become second nature! Title: How to Get Rid of Internet Explorer's Default Internet Speed Monitor Introduction: Internet Explorer (IE) comes with a default internet speed monitor called "Network Activity Indicator" which displays an icon showing the current network activity status in the system tray. While some users find this feature useful, others might consider it annoying or distracting. This tutorial will guide you through disabling the Network Activity Indicator in Windows 7, 8, and 10. Disclaimer: The steps mentioned below are intended only for educational purposes and should be performed at your own risk. We recommend creating a restore point before making any changes to your computer settings. Step 1: Access Taskbar Properties Right-click anywhere on the taskbar and select 'Properties.' A new window named 'Taskbar and Start Menu Properties' will appear. Key tip: Make sure that no programs are currently running as they may interfere with accessing the properties menu. Step 2: Customize Notification Area Icons In the 'Taskbar and Start Menu Properties' window, click on the 'Customize' button located under the 'Notification area' section. Clicking this button opens another window titled 'Notification Area Icons.' Guideline: If the 'Customize' option is grayed out, ensure that the taskbar isn't locked by right-clicking on the taskbar again and deselecting 'Lock all taskbars'. Step 3: Configure Individual Items Scroll down until you locate 'Network' in the list within the 'Behavior' column. You can choose between three options - 'Show icon and notifications', 'Hide icon and notifications,' and 'Only show notifications.' To disable the Network Activity Indicator, change its setting from 'Show icon and notifications' to either 'Hide icon and notifications' or 'Only show notifications.' Explanation: By changing the behavior of the Network item, you control whether or not the Network Activity Indicator appears in the notification area. Hiding the icon completely removes it while displaying only notifications shows the icon briefly when there's network activity but hides it otherwise. Step 4: Save Changes Once you have selected the preferred option, press the 'OK' button at the bottom of both windows to save the changes made. Key Tip: Double-check your selection before saving since these modifications directly affect your user experience. Conclusion: After completing these four simple steps, you successfully disabled the Internet Explorer Network Activity Indicator, freeing up space in your system tray. Note that these actions do not uninstall or remove IE itself; they simply modify one aspect of its functionality. With just bean sprouts and a little bit of um... There's a bunch of me yeah. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Emma. Emma loved to play in the garden. She would run around the garden, picking flowers and finding shells to admire. It's here to protect us." Emma was not content with this answer and said, "But I want to play with it!" Her mother said, "I'm sorry, Emma. The shield is not a toy. It's here to be useful, so it shouldn't be played with." Emma didn't understand. She started to cry and said, "But I just want to have fun." Her mother hugged her and said, "I know, Emma. But the shield is here to help us, not for playing. Sometimes we need to use things for the right reasons, even if we don't understand why." Emma calmed down and realized that her mother was right. She admired the shield, understanding that it was there to be useful and help protect them. The moral of the story is that it's important to use things in the right way, even when we don't understand why. [scary story about bunny being lost, thinking had gone outside, afraid would be hurt. [puts the lid on the box as Lai struggles with it] Title: How to Build a Simple Homemade Electromagnet Building a simple electromagnet at home can be an educational and fun experiment. This guide will walk you through the process of creating your own electromagnet using household items. **Things You'll Need:** 1. A nail (preferably iron or steel) 2. Insulated copper wire (20-gauge is ideal) 3. A battery (9V recommended) 4. Wire strippers 5. Safety glasses 6. Optional: Rubber band or electrical tape **Step 1: Choose Your Nail** The first step in building your homemade electromagnet is choosing the right kind of nail. An iron or steel nail works best because these metals are naturally ferromagnetic, meaning they can become magnetized. Avoid nails made from aluminum, brass, or other non-ferromagnetic materials as they won't work for this project. **Step 2: Prepare Your Copper Wire** Next, prepare your insulated copper wire by stripping about half an inch off both ends of the wire using wire strippers. Make sure not to cut the wire; you just want to expose the metal underneath. The length of the wire depends on how strong you want your electromagnet to be - the longer the wire, the stronger the magnetic field. However, keep in mind that too much wire may cause excessive heat buildup when current flows through it. **Step 3: Wind the Wire Around the Nail** Now, carefully wrap the stripped end of your wire around the center portion of the nail, leaving some room at the top so you can connect it to the battery later. Try to keep the turns close together without overlapping to maximize the magnetic effect. Continue winding until you reach the desired number of turns. For a basic electromagnet, anywhere between 20-50 turns should suffice. **Key Tip**: It's crucial to maintain consistent direction while wrapping the wire. If you reverse the direction halfway through, it could cancel out the magnetic field. To ensure consistency, always wind clockwise or counterclockwise throughout the entire process. **Step 4: Secure the Wire Ends** Once you've finished winding the wire, secure the loose ends either with a rubber band or electrical tape to prevent them from unraveling. **Step 5: Connect the Wire to the Battery** Take one of the stripped wire ends and touch it to the positive terminal of the battery. Then take the other stripped end and touch it to the negative terminal. You should notice a slight tug or resistance indicating that the nail has become magnetized. Congratulations! You've successfully built a simple electromagnet. Remember, once disconnected from the power source, your electromagnet will lose its magnetism rather quickly. But reconnecting it to the battery will restore its magnetic properties instantly. **Guidelines for Safe Handling:** 1. Always wear safety glasses when handling tools like wire strippers to protect your eyes. 2. Be careful not to let the bare wire touches anything else while connected to the battery, especially if children are present, as it could pose an electrocution hazard. 3. Disconnect the wire from the battery before adjusting the coil to avoid damaging the insulation or causing a short circuit. With practice and patience, you can create more powerful electromagnets by increasing the number of wire winds, using larger batteries, or selecting thicker gauge wires. Happy experimenting! Life in the Desert 2nd Grade Oral Language Resources Children will: Learn about the concept of life in the desert. Access prior knowledge and build background about desert animals. Explore and apply the concept of desert animals to why desert animals are able to survive in a desert habitat. Children will: Demonstrate an understanding of deserts. Orally use words that describe what desert animals look like. Extend oral vocabulary by talking about how desert animals are different from other animals. Use key concept words [polar bear, clownfish, rattlesnake, den, reef, the Arctic, habitat]. Explain Use the slideshow to review the key concept words. Explain that children are going to learn about: What a habitat is. What a habitat in the Arctic is like. What a coral reef habitat is like. What a desert habitat is like. Model After the host introduces the slide show, point to the photo on screen. Ask children: What do you see in this picture? (sand, rocks, a few plants). Why is a desert a hard place to live? (really hot, really dry, very little water to drink). Proceed to the next slide. Ask children: Why does a camel's hump help it live in a desert? (helps it go longer without food). Say: The desert seems like a hard place to live. If you lived in the desert, what would you do to be comfortable? (answers will vary). Guided Practice Guide children through the next two slides, showing the different plants and animals that live in the desert. Always have the children explain why that plant or animal is able to live in a desert. Apply Have children play the games that follow. Have them discuss with their partner the different topics that appear during the Talk About It feature. After the first game, ask children to talk about how they can tell which animals can survive in the desert and help them learn more about the animals they're interested in. After the second game, have them discuss whether they would want to live in a desert. Close Ask children: Why is the desert a good habit for some animals? Explain. Summarize for children that a desert is an area that is very hot, very dry, and very sandy. A desert can be a hard place to live, but some plants and animals have special ways to survive in the desert. Encourage children to think about what they would do and see on a trip to a desert. You can think of a class as a mold from which objects are made. The class defines all the properties and methods that its objects will have. Objects created from a class are called instances of the class. A class can "extend" another class, which means that it takes all the properties and methods of the class but can add its own. Title: How to Get Through the Lab in Stick RPG 2 Stick RPG 2 is an online adventure game that allows you to explore a virtual world as a stick figure. One of the challenges in this game is getting through the lab, which can be difficult if you don't know what you're doing. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to get through the lab in Stick RPG 2. Step 1: Prepare Before Entering the Lab Before entering the lab, make sure you have enough money to pay for the entrance fee. The cost varies depending on your intelligence stat, so save up accordingly. You can earn money by working various jobs around the city or by completing quests. Additionally, boost your intelligence stat as high as possible before attempting the lab. A higher intelligence stat will increase your chances of success and reduce the number of attempts needed to pass the lab. You can improve your intelligence by studying at the library or using smart drugs. Key Tip: Save your game before entering the lab so that you can reload if you fail. Step 2: Pass the First Two Rooms Upon entering the lab, you will encounter two rooms filled with questions related to general knowledge and science. To answer these questions correctly, use your real-life knowledge or refer to the internet for help. Each correct answer will give you points towards passing the room. If you accumulate enough points, you will move on to the next room. Guideline: Take your time answering the questions and think carefully about each response. Step 3: Solve Puzzles in the Third Room The third room contains puzzles that require logical thinking and problem-solving skills. These puzzles may include pattern recognition, math problems, or word games. Use your critical thinking skills to solve these puzzles and gain points towards passing the room. Again, if you collect enough points, you will proceed to the final room. Key Tip: Some puzzles have multiple solutions, so try different approaches if you're stuck. Step 4: Complete the Final Challenge In the last room, you will face a series of increasingly difficult mini-games designed to test your reflexes, memory, and coordination. These mini-games may involve pressing buttons in sequence, avoiding obstacles, or memorizing patterns. Perform well in these mini-games to earn points and ultimately pass the lab. Remember to prepare adequately, take your time answering questions and solving puzzles, practice mini-games, and enjoy the rewards upon successful completion. Good luck! Waves are among the most familiar features in the ocean. All waves work similarly, so although we are talking about ocean waves here, the same information would apply to any other waves you might discuss in science classes. Ocean waves transport energy over vast distances, although the water itself does not move, except up and down. This may surprise you, but if you think about it, once you are past the breakers on your raft, you pretty much just bob up and down. (You might drift up the beach .well get to that.) This orbital motion is explained in the figure below: There are waves of all sizes and shapes rolling into the beach at any given time. If theyre not stopped by anything, waves can travel across entire ocean basins and so the waves at your beach might be from a storm half a world away. The most familiar ocean waves are caused by the wind. These are wind-driven waves. This sort of motion is set up anytime two fluids rub together, and remember that the atmosphere is essentially fluid. Waves caused by underwater disturbances such as earthquakes, landslides, or volcanic eruptions are called tsunamis. These waves are typically tens to hundreds of kilometers long. The gravitational pull of the sun and moon on the earth causes the tides which are actually tidal waves. Well get back to that. So we can talk about waves, its important that you know some of their parts. The following list refers to the figure below: crest-the very top of the wave trough-the hollow between two crests wave height-the vertical distance between the top of one wave crest and the bottom of the next trough wavelength-the horizontal distance between any one point on one wave and the corresponding point on the next wave steepness-the ratio of height to length amplitude-the maximum vertical displacement of the sea surface from still water level (half the wave height) period-the time it takes for one complete wavelength to pass a stationary point wave speed-the velocity with which waves travel deep water waves-waves that are in water that is deeper than half their wavelength shallow water waves-waves that are in water that is shallower than 1/20 their wavelength (the important difference on these last two is whether or not the sea floor influences the motion of the wave) One waves motion is completely independent of any other wave motion. When two groups of waves meet, they pass right through each other. This is obvious if you consider light and sound waves. When two people talk or your child has both the TV and the stereo on, you can hear both. One set of sound waves doesnt garble the other. Likewise you can see two objects at the same time. What does happen, though is that waves can either add up or cancel each other out as they pass through one another. This property is called superposition. If a crest from one wave happens to line up with the trough of another, they cancel each other out. This is called destructive interference. If two waves line up crest to crest or trough to trough, they add up. This is called constructive interference. This is why waves at the beach are all different sizes. There are lots of different wave groups coming in, and theyre interfering with each other in different ways. Standing waves result when two equal waves are going in opposite direction and in this case you get the usual up/down motion of the water surface but the waves dont progress. These are common in coastal areas where waves reflect off seawalls, ships hulls, or breakwaters. Theyre also common in swimming pools. A special type of standing wave is a seiche. You can observe this by sloshing around in your bathtub (or, if youre less adventurous try walking with coffee). When you get just the right steady wave frequency going in your tub or your cup, the motion quickly builds up and water or coffee sloshes all over the place. When harbors are designed, care has to be taken to give water built up in seiches some way out other than sloshing up into the first floor condos. Waves Hitting Things When a wave hits a hard vertical surface it is reflected. In other words, the wall pushes the water back just as hard as it got pushed, and sets up waves in the other direction. With constructive interference, you end up with bigger and therefore stronger waves. This is why, in the long run, solid seawalls are not good for saving property from the ocean. You end up creating stronger waves that cause even more erosion. Waves are also refracted. When youre at the beach, it appears as if the waves are mostly coming ashore in a straight line. If those waves were generated all over the place out at sea, how is it theyre all heading the same direction? Here is an example of shallow water waves (waves getting steered by the seafloor). They may come in at an angle, but the side that hits shallow water first gets slowed down by friction and the other side "catches up" bending around until theyre parallel with shore. This is shown in the figure. You have no doubt noticed when you swim in the ocean that you tend to drift down the beach. This is called longshore drift and is a consequence of these refracting waves. Along with you, a lot of sand is getting moved along, and this is one way that our barrier island migrate up and down the coastline. Interestingly, because tsunamis have such long wavelengths, they are shallow water waves and so the seafloor steers them around. This is one reason it is so difficult to predict where these waves will have an impact, even if you know what started them and where. The other amazing thing is that they typically travel about 750 kilometers per hour (or 500 miles per hour)! Because theyre so long and low, its hard to identify one until its close to shore and by then its too late to warn coastal residents. Why is surfing good in some places and lousy elsewhere? To understand this, you need to understand how and why waves break. Youve got lots of waves all nicely refracted and heading into the beach. The ones in front start really getting dragged by the bottom and so they slow down. This allows the ones behind them to ride up their backs. As the distance between the rows of waves decreases, all that wave energy gets condensed into a narrower and narrower space and has to go somewhere, so the wave gets taller. Remember that the waves energy goes around in an orbit under the wave. These taller waves require stronger and bigger orbits, which you notice in the fact that just behind where the waves break, you really get pushed alternately toward shore and away from shore (note that unless its a rip current, these waves are not actually going to push you out to sea---theres just that circular motion going on). Meanwhile, the waves are slowing down still more and at some point, the orbit speed gets ahead of the wave speed, and the wave sort of runs over itself. You see this as the wave cresting, and since the water cant support it, it breaks, releasing all the energy, and propelling your surfboard forward. There are 3 basic types of breaking waves, depending on the type of shoreline theyre hitting. Spilling breakers occur on gently sloping coasts where the waves break slowly and over a long distance, with the crest spilling gently down the front of the wave. Thats what we have here. If the coast is steeper, the waves slow down more quickly and so the crest curls way over the front of the wave and plunges down towards the base---in other words it curls. This is a plunging breaker and is a good surfing wave like youd have in Hawaii. In some cases, where the coastline if very steep, the wave builds up very suddenly and breaks right onto the beach. These are surging breakers. The biggest waves in our oceans are the tides. These are caused by the gravitational forces between the earth and the sun and the moon. The moon has the biggest influence because it is close. It essentially pulls up a bulge in the ocean on the side of the earth closest to it. It actually pulls up the land too, but not as much. There is also a bulge on the side opposite the moon. This one is tougher to understand. Ive heard it explained two ways that seem to help: 1. Because of centrifugal force (more an effect of the earth and moon revolving together than an actual force), the ocean on the side of the earth opposite the moon is sort of thrown outward, like you are when you go around a bend in your car. 2. Imagine a race car, minivan, and bicycle starting a race. All three accelerate, and from the point of view of the minivan, the race car shoots out in front and the bicycle gets left behind. The way they spread out depends on the differences in rate of acceleration. Similarly, the side of the earth nearest the moon gets pulled out harder than the side away from the moon relative to the earth itself. The nearside shoots out ahead, and the backside gets left behind. I dont care which of these you prefer, as long as you get that there is this bulge on BOTH sides of the earth even though the moon is only on one side! So this bulge sort of sits there and we rotate around such that sometimes were under the bulge and sometimes were not. Since it takes 24 hours for the earth to complete a rotation, plus we have to catch up a little because while the earth was rotating, the moon was revolving around the earth, we are directly under a bulge, or experiencing high tide, about every 6 1/2 hours. Twice daily tides like this are called semidiurnal tides. It is also possible to have only one high and one low tide per day. That would be a diurnal tide. Partly this depends on your latitude, but it turns out that some 400 variables go into predicting the tide at any one place, so it isnt nearly this simple. The sun tugs on the oceans too, but since its so far away, it has less influence than the moon. You can see the influence when the moon and sun and earth are all lined up. This would be during a full moon and a new moon. With both the sun and moon pulling the same direction, we get extra high high tides and extra low low tides (a big tidal range). These happen twice a month and are called spring tides. In between these, during the quarter phases of the moon, we get tides with the lowest ranges. These are called neap tides. One of the most interesting things about chance is that to figure out the probability that two things will both happen, one usually multiply their two probabilities together. For example, suppose that one wants to know the probability of rolling two dice and getting a certain combination (it could be two 6s or a 3 then a 5, just any two). The possibility of getting a 3 is one in six ( ), and the possibility of getting a 5 is also one in six, so the chances of getting a 3 then a 5 is = 36. If that number is expressed as somewhere between 0 and 1, it equals 0.027...7, which is fairly low. The possibility of getting a 3, then a 5, and then a 2 would be = 216 or 0.00463, which is a much lower probability. Are you and daddy going to make a picture BRT? The Holy Roman Empire (; ), occasionally but unofficially referred to as the Holy Roman Empire of the German Nation, was a group of regions and free cities in central Europe which all came under the rule of an emperor who was elected by the princes and magistrates of the regions and cities within the empire. When Charlemagne died, his Frankish Empire was given to his children and divided into three different countries: West Francia, Lotharingia and East Francia. The Holy Empire started when Otto I of East Francia became emperor in 962, and it was ended by Napoleon in 1806. The emperors claimed to be heirs of Charlemagne and that the Empire dates from 800, when Charlemagne became Frankish Emperor. I want to get a bean bag frog too Mommy. How about eating some of your potatoes? Rationale: Children have to learn the short u in order to read and spell words. The student will learn to recognize /u/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation and a letter symbol, and then practice finding /u/ in words. Materials: Primary paper; pencil; chart with The nutty bugs on the flying bus drove after the sun; Bud the Sub (book); a picture page with the pictures of a bug, bag, rag, rug, pet, pit, tug, tag; every student also needs a card with an angry child on one side and a happy child on the other side. 1. Introduce the lesson by telling the students that writing and reading is tricky. The hardest part is learning to see and hear the mouth moves. Today we are going to make the mouth move for the short u. These mouth moves will be hard to spot at first, but the more you practice, the easier it will get. 2. Have you ever not gotten what you wanted and said /u/? This is the mouth move we're looking for today. Let's stretch out the /u/ and see if we can say it like how you do when you don't get what you want. I'll try bus, b-u-u-u-u-us. I said /u/ right in the middle. 3. Let's try a tongue twister (on the chart). The nutty bugs on the flying bus drove after the sun. Everyone say this together. Now say it again and this time stretch out the /u/ in the middle of words. The nu-u-u-u-utty bu-u-u-ugs on the flying bu-u-u-u-u-us drove after the su-u-u-u-u-u-un. Try it again, but this time break the /u/ out of the words. The n /u/ tty b /u/ gs on the flying b /u/ s drove after the s /u/ n. Very Good! 4. Have students take out their primary paper and pencil. We can now use the letter u to spell /u/. Let's write it! Start at the fence line, draw down to the sidewalk, curve right, and up to the fence line again. I want to see everyone's u's. After I put a stamp on it, I want you to make one whole row of u's just like it. Every time you see a u in a word you will know to make the /u/ mouth move. 5. Call on the students and tell how they know: Do you hear /u/ in nut or not? Kite or cut? Damp or dump? Bug or bag? Cup or cap? Let's see if you can hear the mouth move /u/ in these words. Remember to show your angry child if you do hear the /u/ and show the happy child if you do not hear the /u/. Give these words one by one to the students to see if they can hear the /u/ in them: the, nutty, bugs, on, the, flying, bus, drove, after, the, sun. 6. Read Bud the Sub and talk about the story. Read it again. This time have the students clap their hands when they hear a word with a /u/ in it. List their words on the board. Now they can write a story about Bud using some of these words. 7. For assessment, distribute the picture page and help students name each word. Ask the students to circle the words that have a /u/ in it. J. Lloyd Eldredge (1995). What's two plus two? What letters are in the alphabet? What's the difference between fiction and nonfiction? It's time for them to learn "how" and "why." How does a plant grow? Why is multiplication useful? Why does the Earth spin? What you should do: Be patient and answer questions whenever possible. If you don't know the answer, look it up to show that adults are also curious and that learning is a nonstop process. What kind of cup do you like?
