Mommy I wanted you to get new cup! view a plan This is a lesson on meter Lesson Plan Subject: Music Lesson Plan Title: Teaching Meter, 1st grade The lesson plan: Materials: tape player, cassette with three songs, chalkboard The teacher will introduce the lesson by asking students, Who likes to dance? How do you know when to move your feet? You have to listen to the beat don't you? The repetition of the beat creates what is known as rhythm. The rhythm is what you are dancing to in music. 1. In songs the rhythm is created by a combination of strong and weak beats. 2. Today we're going to talk about two forms of rhythm. The teacher will begin slapping his thighs and clapping his hands, alternating to form a duple meter. Students will be encouraged to join in. 3. The first is called duple meter. It has a strong beat followed by a weaker beat. While the students continue the cadence the teacher will illustrate duple meter on the chalkboard, marking their time with the lines. This is how we show duple meter. The big line gets the heavy beat, the smaller one gets the weaker beat. 4. The teacher will begin the cassette with Clockwork, the example of duple meter. Students will continue keeping their rhythm. The teacher will again join them to help monitor their tempo. 5. Okay, great. Now we're going to move on to triple meter. The teacher will demonstrate triple meter. When the students catch on the teacher will draw the meter on the board. This is how we show triple meter. 6. The teacher will start the cassette again with Trapeze, the example of triple meter. Students will continue keeping their rhythm. The teacher will again join them to help monitor their tempo. 1. Now I'm going to play another song and I want you to listen for the meter. The teacher will then play the third example, Peter Gabriel's Sledgehammer. 2. Raise your hand if you can tell me which type of meter this is? The teacher will call on a few students for their answers and ask the class if they agree. The song has a strong duple meter rhythm. 3. Who knows the song The More We Get Together? The teacher will use the rote technique to review this song. When the students are ready to sing the song together the teacher will begin the triple meter cadence. When all students join in the teacher will begin the song. 4. At this point, if time permits, the students can be broken into three groups for singing the song in rounds. Despite the fact that students will be at different points in the song their rhythm should be identical. 5. All right, you guys did wonderful. I like the way you followed my directions. Title: How to Find the Surface Area and Volume of a Cuboid A cuboid is a three-dimensional shape that has six rectangular faces. The surface area refers to the total area covered by these six faces, while the volume measures the space occupied by the cuboid. This tutorial will guide you through calculating both the surface area and volume of a cuboid using mathematical formulas. **Step 1: Understand the Basics of a Cuboid** Before diving into calculations, familiarize yourself with the properties of a cuboid: * It has six rectangular faces. * Opposite faces are equal in size (area). * Each face is defined by its length (l), width (w), and height (h). ![Cuboid Diagram](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/d/da/Calculate-the-Surface-Area-and-Volume-of-a-Cuboid-Step-2.jpg/v4-728px-Calculate-the-Surface-Area-and-Volume-of-a-Cuboid-Step-2.jpg) **Step 2: Measure the Length, Width, and Height** To calculate either the surface area or volume, first measure the length (l), width (w), and height (h) of one face of the cuboid accurately. These measurements should be taken in the same unit system (e.g., centimeters, inches, etc.) for consistency during calculation. **Key Tip**: If you only have two dimensions available, assume the third dimension as '1' if you need to find the missing measurement or visualize a complete cuboid. **Step 3: Calculating Surface Area** Use this formula to compute the surface area (SA): SA = 2(lw + lh + wh) Explanation: The surface area consists of twice the sum of areas formed by multiplying any two sides together since there are two pairs of identical faces on opposite sides. Therefore, we add up all possible combinations - length width, length height, and width height - then double the result. For example, let's say our measured lengths are: l=5 cm; w=3 cm; h=4 cm Then SA becomes: SA = 2[(5)(3) + (5)(4) + (3)(4)] = 2[15 + 20 + 12] = 2[47] = 94 square units So, the surface area of the cuboid would be 94 square centimeters when using those given dimensions. **Step 4: Calculating Volume** Utilize this simple formula to determine the volume (V): V = lwh This equation multiplies the length, width, and height together because every point within a cuboid can be reached by moving along each respective axis once from origin to endpoint. Thus, it represents the amount of space enclosed inside the cuboid. Using the same values from Step 3, we get: V = (5)(3)(4) = 60 cubic units Therefore, the volume of the cuboid would be 60 cubic centimeters. **Guidelines:** * Always ensure your measurements are accurate. * Use consistent units across all dimensions during calculations. * Remember the difference between surface area and volume - surface area deals with just the skin/covering of an object whereas volume concerns internal space occupied. Shake that Shhhh! Rationale: For children to be about to read and spell words, first they must be able to recognize letters. Once the student achieves letter recognition, they move on to phoneme blends and diagraphs. This lesson will help child with the diagraph /sh/ in reading, writing and spelling words. 1. Poster paper 2. "Shelly should shake the ship" written on the 3. Marker for the poster paper 5. Letters for the letter boxes 6. One Fish, Two Fish, Red Fish, Blue Fish by: Dr. Seuss Random House Books for Young Readers; 1988 9. An assessment sheet for each student 1.The lesson will help us understand how we can make one sound by joining two letters together. "When a baby is crying or you want someone to be quiet, what do we say? We tell them 'shhhhh' don't we? Yes, so when we make that sound, what are your teeth doing? Right. They're together. Also, you blow air out of your mouth. Did you know that when you say 'shh' you are saying two letters to make one sound." 2."Now we're going to say a tongue twister. Follow along as I read this saying. Shelly should shake the ship. Let's say it again, but this time we're going to stretch out the /sh/ sound in each word. Ok, this last time, we're going to separate the /sh/ sound from the rest of this word. So instead of Shelly we will say Sh-elly. Okay, let's go. Sh-elly sh-ould sh-ake the sh-ip." 3.Now we are going to use individual letters and boxes to spell words with the sh digraph. When we spell words using our letterboxes we need to remember that, only one sound can go into each box. "So, how many boxes I need for the word she?" Two that is right! "Can you tell me the two sounds? Exactly, /sh/ and /E/" 4."Can you tell me how many sounds are in shed? Shed has three sounds which are /sh/, /e/, and /d/. (Model this for students on the marker board) "In the first box we will put the /sh/ sound which would be the letters s and h. In the second box we will put the /e/ sound which would be the letter e. In the third box we will put the /d/ sound which would be the letter d. Now I am going to say several words with the sh digraph in them and I want you to try to spell them using your letters and letterboxes." 5.Have your students spell the following words: (2) she, (3 boxes) fish, dish, shell, (4 boxes) splash. As student is spelling the words, make sure that each letter is being placed in the correct letter box. After students have practiced spelling out the words, have them read the following words from a list: wish, she, share, crash, bush, mash. 6. Now have your class read One Fish, Two Fish, Red Fish, Blue Fish by: Dr. Seuss. "As we are reading this book, I want you to look for the words that have the /sh/ sound." Guitar-like plucked string instruments have been used for many years. In many countries and at many different time periods, guitars and other plucked string instruments have been very popular, because they are light to carry from place to place, they are easier to learn to play than many other instruments. Guitars are used for many types of music, from Classical to Rock. Most pieces of popular music that have been written since the 1950s are written with guitars. The steak is better than the rice. The Arctic Ocean is the ocean around the North Pole. The most northern parts of Eurasia and North America are around the Arctic Ocean. Thick pack ice and snow cover almost all of this ocean in winter, and most of it in summer. An icebreaker or a nuclear-powered submarine can use the Northwest Passage through the Arctic Ocean to go between the Pacific and Atlantic oceans. You going to say their grace for 'em? Uh just to study my uh spelling words? you are here: home > explore > coverings There are several layers of tissue that separate your brain from the outside world. First, there is your skin (scalp). Beneath the skin is bone (your skull). Below the skull are three special coverings called the meninges. You may have heard of the illness called meningitis. Meningitis is an infection of the meninges. The outer layer of the meninges is called the dura mater or just the dura. The dura is tough and thick and it can restrict the movement of the brain within the skull. This protects the brain from movements that may stretch and break brain blood vessels. The middle layer of the meninges is called the arachnoid. The inner layer, the one closest to the brain, is called the pia mater or just the pia. Here is an easy way to remember the order of the meninges: "The meninges PAD the brain." -- Pia; Arachnoid; Dura. Did you know? For more information about the meninges, see: Copyright 1996-2008, Eric H. Chudler All Rights Reserved. Merwan had a normal childhood and liked poetry and sports, especially cricket. When he was thirteen years old he started a boy's club with his best school friends called "The Cosmopolitan Club". The boys in the club kept up on the news, practiced public speaking at their clubhouse, and raised money to give to the poor. He graduated from St. Vincent's High School and attended Deccan College. In 1913, when Baba was nineteen, he was coming home from college riding a bicycle. A very old Muslim woman named Hazrat Babajan, referred to as a Perfect Master (God Realized), was sitting under a Neem tree, called for him to come over to her, he said he was drawn to her like iron to a magnet. He began spending time visiting with her and serving her. In 1914 she kissed him on the forehead, he then went home. Baba later said that he became so dazed after this kiss that he could barely find his way home, and that when Babajan kissed him, initially he lost all consciousness of the world, he then realized God within himself. He was so dazed he neither ate or slept for several weeks. Moving about semi-autonomously in 1915 Merwan was drawn to the other five Perfect Masters, beginning with Sai Baba of Shirdi who upon seeing Merwan called out to him "Parvardigar!" (Divine Sustainer). Upasni Maharaj, also a Perfect Master on seeing Merwan thru a stone, hitting him on the forehead where Babajan had kissed Merwan. He often went to live with Upasni at Sakori. The five Masters assisted Merwan in regaining his integration of the physical world of duality with the Divine Oneness that is the Avatar's natural state. Merwan served as their disciple while in fact he was already fully God Realized. oh, do you want to sit up there to color? Sharks have tiny holes all over the shark's snout, especially between the eye and the tip of the snout. In them are nerve receptors which are called the "ampullae of Lorenzini." They can sense electricity in the water. Animals in the water give off electricity: every time an animal's heart beats or it moves, tiny currents of electricity are made. These tiny electric currents make signals that travel through water and get sensed. Sharks may use this sense when they catch their prey, even more than they use their sight. She sat between the "Reform Mayor," whose guest she was, and the "Militant Editor," neither in the highest spirits after their recent and unexpected defeat; and heard much of that intimate political talk for which she had longed, although her mind wandered occasionally to that romantic past of caballero and do  a not yet a century old, very difficult to conjure in this swarming heterogeneous valley. She offered to furnish a room immediately, and Miss Montgomery smilingly promised to obtain surcease from dinner-parties, where her portion was to enter by the back door--in nine cases out of ten with the ancestral silver--and take the rest she needed. February 12th.--It's awful hard work to grind away like this--as I have the last two days. Volap k is a constructed language created in 1880 by Johann Martin Schleyer. Schleyer was a Catholic priest from Germany. He felt that God had told him in a dream to make an international language. Volap k conventions took place in 1884, 1887, and 1889. The aim was to help people from different cultures speak to each other. You want' to go play in the water? Title: How to Avoid Getting Your Image Deleted from wikiHow Introduction: wikiHow is a collaborative community that strives to create high-quality, informative content illustrated with relevant images. However, sometimes user-submitted images may be flagged for deletion due to various reasons such as copyright issues, low quality, or irrelevance. This guide will help you avoid getting your image deleted by providing clear steps, helpful tips, and essential guidelines. Step 1: Understand Copyright Laws Before submitting an image to wikiHow, ensure that you have obtained proper permission to use it. If the image features people or private property, obtain their consent before uploading. Failing to respect copyright laws can lead to immediate removal of your image and possible consequences beyond wikiHow. Key tip: Use original images taken by yourself whenever possible, ensuring complete control over rights and permissions. Step 2: Choose Relevant Images Select images directly related to the article topic. Irrelevant images confuse users and distract them from the main subject matter. An appropriate image should visually enhance understanding of the accompanying text. Guideline: Review the wikiHow Image Upload Policy (<https://www.wikihow.com/Image-Upload-Policy>) for more information about suitable subjects. Step 3: Maintain High Quality Submitting blurry, pixelated, or otherwise poor-quality images harms readability and detracts from the overall appearance of the page. Always strive to provide crisp, well-lit photos that accurately depict the described actions. Key tip: For best results, utilize good lighting conditions when taking pictures and consider investing in a decent camera if necessary. Step 4: Provide Proper Credit When using someone else's work, always give credit where it's due. Cite sources accurately and completely according to wikiHow's citation standards (<https://www.wikihow.com/Help:Citing>). Failure to attribute properly could result in image deletion. Step 5: Follow Fair Use Guidelines Fair use allows limited usage of copyrighted material without requiring explicit permission under specific circumstances like education, news reporting, criticism, etc. Make sure your use case complies with these restrictions to minimize potential legal disputes. Consult resources like Stanford University Libraries' Copyright & Fair Use site (<http://fairuse.stanford.edu/>) for further guidance. Step 6: Optimize File Size Large files slow down load times and negatively impact user experience. Compress images using free tools like TinyPNG (<https://tinypng.com/>) while preserving visual fidelity. Most modern devices automatically save compressed versions of images, so check settings prior to capturing new photos. Step 7: Adhere to Community Standards Respect fellow contributors and adhere to community norms regarding language, behavior, and contributions. Refrain from posting offensive, violent, sexually suggestive, discriminatory, or illegal content. Violating community standards might lead to image removal and other penalties. Step 8: Participate Actively Engage with the wikiHow community through constructive discussions, editing articles, improving existing images, and sharing knowledge. Active participants demonstrate dedication and commitment to enhancing the platform, making them less likely targets for image deletions. Conclusion: By following this comprehensive guide, you significantly reduce the chances of having your images removed from wikiHow. Here's a number line, counting, sorting, matching maths resource with a strong transport-vehicles-trucks theme. The 4 page pdf download includes a series of truck images (10 trucks per A4 page). The image quality is high enough so that the pages could be enlarged if necessary. The trucks feature the following: - spots (0 to 12); - the numerals 0 to 12; - the written numerals 'zero' to 'twelve'; - the ordinals 1st... to 10th The A4 pages should be cut into separate cards and can then be used as a number line, or display. They can be 'pegged' on a washing line, or mixed and sorted. Here are a few more ideas: Pick up a small number of beads, match the beads to the spots on a truck. "Which truck can you 'fill'? Can you fill more than one truck?" Ask the children to count the spots on each truck. Can they put two of the trucks next to each other (in convoy) so that they show 5 spots altogether? Can they choose two trucks to make 6, or 7? With laminated cards, connect pairs of spots on the trucks to find which trucks have even and which ones have odd numbers Cover part of a spotty truck with a counter, tell the children how many spots there should be in total and ask them how many must be under the counter. "Here's a truck with 9 spots... how many are hidden?" Mix the spotty trucks and the number trucks together as one pack - play snap or pelmanism games Mix up (say) the 1 to 4 spotty trucks on the table, let the children look at them for a moment. Ask the children to close their eyes and then you hide one of the trucks. "Open your eyes... can you work out which truck has driven away? ... How do you know?" Can the children match the 1 to 4 spotty trucks to the number trucks or to the written numeral - or both? Put a mixed up selection of trucks (numbers, spots, written numerals) on a washing line and challenge the children to arrange them in order. Use the ordinal numbers on the trucks to demonstrate a race - give a group of children a truck each and ask them to line up in 'order'. Note: we switched the orientation of the trucks around for the ordinal numbers so the order has a more logical feel (left to right) - but children need experiences that involve ordering items in both horizontal directions - and vertically too. The images are high quality so you can print them larger than they appear in the pdf here or use them on an IWB. Title: How to Deal with Cheap Friends Introduction: Friendships are built on mutual respect, trust, and understanding. However, sometimes you may encounter friends who are cheap or overly frugal, which can lead to uncomfortable situations and strain your relationship. This guide will provide you with steps and strategies to deal with cheap friends while maintaining a healthy friendship. Step 1: Understand Their Perspective Before addressing any issues related to their frugality, try to understand why they might be acting this way. It could stem from financial difficulties, past experiences, cultural backgrounds, or personal beliefs about money management. By empathizing with them, you'll be better equipped to approach the situation without causing offense. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Remember that everyone has different attitudes towards money based on upbringing, values, and circumstances. * Avoid making assumptions; instead, engage in open communication to clarify their behavior. Step 2: Set Boundaries Determine what behaviors make you feel uncomfortable and establish boundaries accordingly. For example, if you find it awkward when they never contribute to group outings, let them know politely but firmly that such actions aren't fair to others involved. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be clear about your expectations and limitations. * Use "I" statements to express your feelings rather than blaming or accusing language. Step 3: Communicate Openly Talk to your friend about your concerns in a non-confrontational manner. Explain how their frugality affects you and propose potential solutions. Make sure to listen actively to their side as well. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Choose an appropriate time and place for the conversation. * Focus on specific incidents and avoid generalizations. * Stay calm and composed during the discussion. Step 4: Suggest Alternatives Offer alternatives that cater to both parties' needs. If going to expensive restaurants frequently is straining your budget, suggest cheaper eateries, potluck dinners at home, or outdoor activities instead. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Think creatively and consider low-cost options. * Show appreciation for their willingness to compromise. Step 5: Educate Yourself About Finances Learn more about personal finance so you can have informed discussions about spending habits and budget constraints. Sharing knowledge might help alleviate some tension and foster mutual understanding. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Read books, articles, or attend workshops on financial literacy. * Share resources with your friend to encourage learning together. Step 6: Encourage Healthy Financial Habits Support your friend's efforts to save money by sharing deals, discounts, or budgeting apps. Also, remind them gently that true relationships value shared experiences beyond monetary worth. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Promote balance between saving and enjoying life. * Highlight the importance of building meaningful memories together. Step 7: Seek Professional Help (If Necessary) If your friend's extreme frugality stems from deeper psychological issues like hoarding disorder or obsessive compulsive personality disorder (OCPD), recommend seeking professional assistance. A mental health expert can provide guidance and treatment options. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Recognize signs of disordered thinking regarding finances. * Offer support during their journey toward recovery. Conclusion: Navigating through a friendship with someone who is excessively frugal requires patience, tact, and compassion. Following these steps will enable you to maintain a strong bond while ensuring equal contributions and minimizing resentment. Who walks pitter-patter down the garden? He can't take that in or the little stickers will come off. We only spank little babies when they don't listen. And he'll, you know sit there and babble as if he's like reading it to me. For years, experts suspected there might be water on the moon, but there was no way for them to prove it. New images sent from a spacecraft orbiting the moon may finally show what experts have hoped to see. The Moon Mineralogy Mapper, or M3, surveyed the moon's polar regions. M3's maps, along with the help of two other spacecraft, confirm that much more water may exist on the moon than experts thought. Anywhere water is found in the universe, whether on the moon or on Mars, could mean that life as we know it may also exist there. On Earth, water is essential to life. Life forms such as bacteria, plants, and animals all depend on water. In this lesson, you will get a look at the cycle of water-from rain to ground and back up into clouds. You will also find out how New York City gets its daily water supply. Before you get started, stop and think about water in your own life. By yourself or with one or more classmates, brainstorm a list of ways you use water. Make another list of places you have seen water. Keep these lists handy to use later. The Water Cycle Begin your journey with Thirstin's Water Cycle. Read along while you listen with your computer's speakers or using headphones. Click Rain to get the cycle started. After you watch this segment, think of a time and place that you remember it raining. On a blank piece of letter-sized paper, draw a picture of what that looked like. Show what happened to the water after it landed on the Earth's surface. For example, did it run into a sewer drain or through a rain gutter? Did some of it pool up in a dish or collect in a pond? Label this drawing with the word "Precipitation." Now, click Water Storage. Using the animation as your guide, draw another picture that shows what the rain you saw may have looked like underground. Also, show examples of two or more reasons people would want to have stored water nearby. Drinking it, giving it to pets or livestock, bathing, or watering house plants are some common uses. Label this drawing "Water Storage." In the Vapor part of the cycle, the liquid form of water turns into steam, which is the gas form of water. What is the sun's job here? Draw a picture showing what happens during this phase of the cycle, and label it "Transpiration." Draw one more picture to show what happens to the water vapor in the Clouds. Label this drawing "Condensation." Together with your classmates, post your Rain drawings in one central area on your classroom wall. As you post your own, tell your classmates about that memory of when it rained. Listen to others as they share their memories. Next, post your Water Storage drawing on another area of the wall, but near the Rain area. As you post your picture, describe it to your classmates. Post your Vapor drawings near those, and then the Clouds pictures. Ask four of your classmates to volunteer (or volunteer yourself) to each make an arrow to post on the wall. They should post an arrow between each part of the water cycle to make a full circle. Review the cycle together with classmates. Talk about how you think the sun affects the water cycle in each part of the circle. Also, discuss why you sometimes see clouds without precipitation falling. What do you think happens when it snows? Flowing through New York City In modern life, a droplet moving through the water cycle sometimes gets detoured during its journey. To get an idea what these detours might be, check out The New York City Water Story. Be prepared to sketch out the journey as you read and listen to the story. Read the introduction, and then click Begin. Choose To the Faucet and trace the city's water source. After the first screen, continue into the Watershed and then head to the Reservoirs. In your journey sketch, note how many reservoirs New York City taps into for its water and how many gallons the city uses every year. The water travels through the Aqueducts & Tunnels to reach the facility for Keeping It Clean, before flowing out of Your Faucet. How many gallons of water are delivered to the city every day? What are some ways to conserve this clean water? Next, head Down the Drain to follow where used water goes. You will make a stop at the Wastewater Treatment plant. What else may get mixed into Stormwater besides water? What can happen when Combined Sewer Overflow moves faster than the treatment process? Finally, find out What You Can Do About Pollution. Now, get ready to write your own story, as a drop of water. Review the two lists you made at the start of this lesson. Choose one use of water and one place you or a classmate has seen water. Use your water cycle drawings and your New York City Water Story sketch to help you imagine your journey, from rain to ground to use and back up into a cloud. As you create the story, make sure to weave in the water use and place you chose. Add drawings to help show what happens to you along the way. Share your story with others. Find examples of the water cycle and water uses in issues of the e-edition. Print or cut out photos, news articles, weather forecasts, or feature stories that show or describe these examples. Post each example on the classroom wall with the water cycle part that it matches. For water uses, start a new posting area to display all of those examples together. Can you put the box on the doll? Title: How to Draw a Small Map Drawing a small map can be a useful skill for planning out a room layout, creating a simple game map, or visualizing a space for a story. This tutorial will guide you through the process of drawing a basic small map, providing clear and practical steps with detailed explanations along the way. **Materials Needed:** 1. Paper (preferably grid paper) 2. Pencil 3. Eraser 4. Ruler 5. Pen or fine-liner (for final touches) **Step 1: Choose Your Subject and Scale** Before starting your map, decide what subject you want to draw. Common subjects include building floor plans, outdoor areas, or fictional locations. Next, determine the scale of your map by deciding how many units (inches, centimeters, etc.) represent a certain distance within your map. For example, if you want one inch to equal ten feet, then mark this ratio on your paper as a reference. *Key tip*: Make sure your chosen scale allows enough space to accurately depict all necessary details while keeping your map small. **Step 2: Sketch Out Basic Structures** Using light pencil strokes, begin sketching the main structures or landmarks present in your scene. These could include walls, doors, trees, buildings, or other prominent features. Use your ruler to ensure straight lines and maintain consistency throughout your map. *Guideline*: Focus on major shapes first; smaller elements can be added later. **Step 3: Add Secondary Features** Once primary structures are established, incorporate secondary features such as furniture, windows, paths, rivers, or other relevant objects that help define the area further. Keep referring back to your chosen scale to ensure accurate proportions. *Tip*: Try using dashed lines for non-solid boundaries like tree lines or fences. **Step 4: Label Key Areas** Label important locations clearly using capital letters without obscuring any part of your map. Examples might include rooms, specific buildings, geographical points, or terrain types. Consider including a legend or key explaining any symbols used in your map. *Note*: Be mindful of readability - avoid cramping labels together or making them too small to decipher. **Step 5: Refine Lines and Check Accuracy** Go over your initial sketches with darker pen or fineliner strokes where needed. Double-check measurements and positions against your original scale and make corrections accordingly. Don't forget to erase unnecessary pencil marks at this stage. *Hint*: Using different line weights can emphasize various aspects of your map, guiding viewers' eyes towards crucial information. **Step 6: Final Review and Adjustments** Review your map carefully, checking for errors, inconsistencies, or omissions. Make adjustments as required until satisfied with the overall appearance and accuracy. Remember, simplicity is key when creating a small map. Too much detail may clutter the image and hinder understanding. *Advanced Tip*: If desired, add shading or color to enhance visual appeal and clarify spatial relationships between elements. Congratulations! You now have a completed small map ready for use. Whether you plan to navigate an imaginary world, rearrange office spaces, or design dungeons for tabletop games, your newly acquired cartography skills should serve you well. Happy mapping! = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV3/ER/kever3.cha = = = Only if someone was to come across with another magnet and go like this. You want to make the teddy bear look funny? Explaining a Gluten Sensitivity to a Child Gluten is a protein found in certain grains such as wheat, barley, and rye. Some people have difficulty digesting gluten, leading to various health issues. This tutorial will help you explain gluten sensitivity to a child in an easy-to-understand way while providing key tips and guidelines along the way. **Step 1: Define what gluten is** Start by explaining that gluten is a type of protein found in some foods like bread, pasta, cookies, cakes, and cereals made with wheat, barley, or rye. You can compare it to other proteins they might know, like those found in meat, eggs, or milk. *Key tip*: Use simple language and examples relevant to their daily life. For instance, mention specific food items they consume regularly containing gluten. **Step 2: Describe how our bodies process food** Help them understand how our body digests food. When we eat, our stomach breaks down food into smaller parts called nutrients so our bodies can absorb them and use them for energy, growth, and repair. Sometimes, though, our bodies may react negatively to certain nutrients due to sensitivities or allergies. *Guideline*: Relate this concept to something familiar, perhaps a toy they take apart and reassemble, drawing parallels between breaking down toys (food) into pieces (nutrients). **Step 3: Introduce the idea of gluten sensitivity** Now, introduce the term 'gluten sensitivity.' Explain that when someone has a gluten sensitivity, their body finds it hard to break down and absorb gluten properly, causing discomfort or illness. Unlike celiac disease or a wheat allergy, which are more severe reactions, gluten sensitivity doesn't damage the intestines but still makes one feel unwell. *Teaching moment*: Compare it to eating too much candy (simple carbs), making them feel sluggish afterward because their body struggles to handle all the sugar at once. **Step 4: Discuss symptoms associated with gluten sensitivity** List common symptoms related to gluten sensitivity, including bloating, diarrhea, constipation, headaches, fatigue, skin rashes, joint pain, and brain fog. Remember to emphasize these vary from person to person; therefore, not everyone experiences the same symptoms. *Important note*: Be sensitive about discussing bodily functions, reassuring them that it's normal and part of being human. **Step 5: Highlight the importance of a gluten-free diet** Once they grasp the concept of gluten sensitivity, discuss the solution - following a gluten-free diet. Emphasize that avoiding foods with gluten can significantly improve how someone with gluten sensitivity feels. Provide alternatives like rice, corn, potatoes, quinoa, buckwheat, etc., which don't contain gluten. *Practical advice*: Encourage reading labels together to identify hidden sources of gluten in processed foods. **Step 6: Reiterate the prevalence of gluten sensitivity** To avoid stigmatizing those who must adhere to a gluten-free lifestyle, remind them that many people live happily without gluten. It's just another variation in dietary needs, similar to lactose intolerance or vegetarianism. *Positive spin*: Focus on the variety of delicious naturally gluten-free options available! By following these steps, you should be able to clearly convey the concept of gluten sensitivity to a child, helping them empathize with individuals managing this condition. Will we turn the page in a minute? Like some kids are just really into stuffed animals. Determining the sex of parakeets can be a challenging but exciting task! With careful observation and attention to detail, you can accurately determine whether your parakeet is male or female. This guide will walk you through various methods used to sex parakeets, providing detailed explanations, key tips, and helpful guidelines along the way. **Method 1: Vent Sexing by an Avian Veterinarian** Step 1: Consult with an avian veterinarian. This method should only be performed by experienced professionals as it requires handling the bird gently yet firmly. It involves visual examination of the cloaca (the common opening for the reproductive, urinary, and intestinal tracts) to identify sexual organs. Why this works: In adult birds, males have two pointed horns called papillae protruding from their cloacae, while females do not. However, these structures are internal in juvenile birds, making vent sexing difficult until they reach maturity at around 3-4 months old. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Always consult a certified avian vet for accurate results. * Avoid attempting vent sexing yourself if you lack experience, as improper handling may harm the bird. **Method 2: Color-based Sexual Dimorphism** Step 1: Observe your parakeet's feather colors. Some color mutations exhibit sexual dimorphism - differences in appearance between males and females. For instance, in budgerigars (Melopsittacus undulatus), certain mutations like the Yellowface, Olive, and Grey Green display distinct color variations based on gender. Why this works: The genes responsible for specific colors are linked to sex chromosomes, resulting in different appearances between males and females. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Familiarize yourself with various mutations and corresponding sex-linked traits. * Note that not all mutations show clear sexual dimorphism; some require additional methods for confirmation. **Method 3: Behavioral Differences** Step 1: Monitor your parakeet's behavior. Males often display more aggressive behaviors such as chasing, biting, and regurgitating food during courtship. Females tend to be calmer and quieter, spending more time preening and less time engaging in territorial disputes. Why this works: These behaviors are driven by hormonal differences between males and females, although individual personalities may vary. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Consider multiple factors when assessing behavior, including age, environment, socialization, and overall health. * Keep in mind that behavior alone might not provide definitive proof of sex. **Method 4: Vocalizations** Step 1: Listen carefully to your parakeet's vocalizations. Male parakeets typically sing more frequently than females, producing melodious tunes using their syrinx (avian voice box). Females make shorter, sharper calls, especially when seeking attention or feeling distressed. Why this works: Again, hormonal influences contribute to these differences in vocal patterns. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Pay close attention to the duration, pitch, and tone of your parakeet's sounds. * Recognize that individual variation exists within both genders, so rely on several methods together for accuracy. By combining these techniques, you increase the likelihood of correctly determining the sex of your parakeet. Remember always to prioritize your pet's wellbeing, consulting professionals whenever necessary and treating them with kindness and respect regardless of gender. Your child will use his knowledge of numeric value and combine it with strategy in order to win the game. An intact male bovine is called a bull. A young male bovine is called a bullock. A mature female that has given birth to at least one or two calves is called a cow. A young bovine between birth and weaning is called a calf. Two or more of these young bovines are calves. A female that has never had a calf is called a heifer, (pronounced "heffer"). Calving is the act of a cow or heifer giving birth to a calf. Title: How to Color a Comic Book **Introduction:** Coloring a comic book is an art form that requires creativity, patience, and attention to detail. It's more than just filling in spaces with colors; it involves understanding color theory, creating moods, enhancing storytelling through visuals, and ensuring technical quality. This guide will take you through the process step-by-step. **Step 1: Understand the Artwork and Storyline** Before you start applying any colors, familiarize yourself with the artwork and understand the narrative. Discuss with the illustrator or writer about their vision for the piece. The coloring should complement the line art and contribute to the overall storytelling. *Key Tip:* Remember, your job as a colorist isn't merely aesthetic - it plays a crucial role in setting tone, revealing character traits, indicating time periods, etc. **Step 2: Choose Your Medium** Decide whether you want to digitally color your comic (using software like Adobe Photoshop or Procreate) or traditionally color it by hand (with markers, colored pencils, watercolors, etc.). Each medium has its pros and cons, so choose based on personal preference, budget, and time constraints. **Step 3: Create a Flat Color Layer** This initial layer serves to separate different elements within the image using distinct flat colors without shading or highlights yet. Use solid, bright hues for easy identification later when adding shadows and lights. *Guideline:* Avoid overly dark tones at this stage because they may interfere with seeing the details clearly during the next steps. **Step 4: Plan Light Sources** Determine where light sources are located within the scene. They significantly influence how objects appear regarding contrast, shadow placement, and reflection. Consistent lighting maintains realism and continuity throughout the comic. **Step 5: Add Shadows** Create a new layer beneath your flats and name it 'Shadows'. Select a darker shade of the base color and paint over areas receiving less light. Blend these shadows smoothly into the adjacent lighter areas. *Key Tip:* For realistic results, remember that shadows often have cool undertones due to absorbing warm light. **Step 6: Apply Highlights** On a new layer above your flats called 'Highlights', pick a lighter version of the original color and highlight prominent points catching direct light. Be careful not to overdo it; subtlety goes a long way here. **Step 7: Render Details** Now, render textures, patterns, and other intricate details onto another new layer named 'Details'. You can also adjust the opacity of this layer if needed. **Step 8: Check Consistency** Ensure consistency across all pages by checking back regularly to previous ones while working. Maintain similar color palettes, lighting conditions, and rendering styles. **Step 9: Final Touches & Special Effects** Add final touches such as weather effects, glow effects, etc., depending on the context. These small additions greatly enhance the overall look and feel of the comic. **Step 10: Review and Export** Proofread thoroughly, looking out for errors, inconsistencies, or areas needing improvement. Once satisfied, export the file according to print specifications or digital platform requirements. Remember, mastering the craft takes practice. Don't be discouraged if your first few attempts don't turn out perfect. Keep experimenting until you find a workflow that suits you best. Happy coloring! mhm, you don't really use it to eat with. The area of a shape is the total number of square units that fill the shape. When we divide a rectangle into squares with side length one, we see that the width is the number of square units in a row and the height is the total number of rows. Thus, the total number of squares inside the rectangle is equal to the number of rows times the number of units in each row, or the height times the width of the rectangle. Area of a Rectangle = (h w) , where h is the height and w is the width. In the sample rectangle from the previous section, h = 3 and w = 6 , so the area is 3 6 = 18 . In other words, there are 18 squares in the interior of the rectangle: When we divide a square into squares with side length one, we see that the number of square units in a row is the same number of total rows, or that h = w . Thus, the area of a square is equal to the length of a side times the length of a side, or to the length of a side squared (the term squared is no coincidence). Area of a Square = s 2 , where s is the length of a side. In the sample square from the previous section, s = 8 , so the area is 82 = 64 . In a square of side length 3, the area is 32 = 9 : And then in the morning she died. oh, there was a lot of people in line and your legs got tired of standing up? The kitten and the hen were not his mother the dog and the cow were not his mother did he have a mother? Welcome to the first of many Project Corner posts! I hope to share projects that you can do at home with your kids. From tots to teens, I'll try to find things to do that are fun for the whole family, and are sometimes educational! Today's post is a fun experiment that's easy, has great results and you probably have everything you need on hand. The Celery Leaves Experiment Preschool to Elementary at least 4 celery ribs, with leafy tops at least 4 different food colors (green isn't really suited for this). 4 glasses, jars, vases, whatever you have water for each glass Cut the ends of your celery ribs. It really doesn't matter how long they are, just as long as they don't tip your glasses over! Set your glasses out and fill each one about 3/4 of the way with water. Tap water is just fine. Add your food color, a few drops will do. Feel free to mix up the colors, and have the kids help decide what to do! This is their project after all! Set the celery ribs in the water Let them sit for 24 hours. What you've learned: Besides a really neat trick, you just taught your kids what osmosis is (google it up!). How you explain that will all depend on the age of your kids. For younger kids, you can keep it simple and explain that plants drink the water through their roots, and that's why the leaves changed! The celery drank up the colored water, the veins carried it up through the stalk into the leaves. For older kids, who may already be learning some of this stuff in early science, this project will reinforce what they might have already learned in the class room. See if they can tell you what happened. If you really want to get into the science aspect, you can have your kids hypothesize about what will happen to the leaves before you start the project. Get a piece of paper and have them write down some predictions (or write it for them). Good questions to start with: What do you think will happen to the leaves? Which color will work best? How long do you think it will take? What do you think will happen to the water? And anything else you can all come up with together. When the project is complete, go back and compare answers to actual results. Did the kids make good predictions? If not, what happened? See if you can explain where things went wrong, or have them try and figure it out! Save this project for a rainy day and you're sure to keep them preoccupied when you can keep them outside! Even I couldn't help but check on the leaves every so often. 24 hours will give you good results, but feel free to keep them in the water for longer and see what happens. If you try this experiment at home, let us know how it goes! A couple more process pics. I LOVE the way the colorful water looks when it catches the morning sunlight. I might just have to fill up some glass bottles and keep them on the windowsill! "Bring another candle," called Morel. Annie and Arthur went. Paul followed with his mother. He stood with his arm round her waist in the inner doorway. Down the middle of the cleared room waited six chairs, face to face. I don't know if it's going to stay! Creating a folder (also known as "labels" in Gmail and Google Inbox) can help you organize your emails more effectively. This tutorial will guide you through creating a new label in Google Inbox. **Step 1: Open Google Inbox** To begin, open your web browser and go to <https://inbox.google.com/>. If you are already logged into your Google account, this should take you directly to the Google Inbox dashboard. If not, enter your email address and password when prompted. **Step 2: Navigate to Labels Section** Once you're on the main screen of Google Inbox, look at the left-hand side panel. You'll see several options like "Inbox," "Sent," "Drafts," etc. Scroll down until you find the section labeled "Labels." Click on it to expand the menu. Creating labels is all about categorizing and organizing your emails under different headers, making them easier to locate later. By navigating to the 'Labels' section, we prepare ourselves for setting up these categories. **Step 3: Select Create New Label Option** After clicking on 'Labels,' a submenu will appear with existing labels if any. At the bottom of this list, click on "+Create new label." This action opens a dialog box where you'll input details for your new label. **Step 4: Name Your Label** In the text field provided in the dialog box, type the name you want for your label. The name could be anything that makes sense to you - clients, work projects, personal, shopping, or whatever suits your needs best. Make sure it's descriptive enough so you can easily identify its purpose later. Key Tip: Avoid using special characters in your label names as they may cause issues while syncing across devices. **Step 5: Nest Your Label (Optional)** If you wish to nest your label within another one (for example, having a subcategory), check the box next to "Nest label under..." Then select the parent label from the dropdown menu. Many users prefer flat structures without nested labels for simplicity, but it depends on individual preferences and organizational styles. **Step 6: Save Your Label** Finally, once you've named your label and decided whether or not to nest it, hit the blue "Create" button at the bottom right corner of the dialog box. Congratulations! You have successfully created a new label in Google Inbox! Now whenever you receive an email related to this category, simply hover over the mail subject line, click on the three vertical dots that appear, then choose "Label," and finally select the appropriate label. Similarly, you can filter old mails and assign them to the newly created label. Remember, organization is key to productivity. So don't hesitate to create more labels as needed, but try to maintain balance; too many labels might become overwhelming and counterproductive. Title: How to Compliment a Man Who Catches Your Eye Introduction: Complimenting someone is a great way to show appreciation and acknowledge their positive qualities or efforts. When you compliment a man who catches your eye, it can help break the ice, start a conversation, and even build his confidence. However, giving genuine compliments requires thoughtfulness and sincerity. This tutorial will guide you through the process of crafting and delivering an effective compliment that makes both of you feel good. Step 1: Observe Attentively Key Tip: Pay attention to what stands out about him - his appearance, personality traits, or actions. Before offering a compliment, take time to observe the person carefully. Look for unique features, such as clothing style, facial hair, or body language, which distinguish him from others. Notice if he displays any admirable character traits like kindness, humor, or intelligence. Also, consider his achievements and contributions. By focusing on specific aspects of his persona, you demonstrate authentic interest and respect. Step 2: Craft a Personalized Compliment Key Guideline: Make sure the compliment is relevant, sincere, and tailored to the individual. A well-crafted compliment should be personalized, addressing particular attributes or behaviors observed during observation. Avoid generic comments like "you're cute" or "you have nice eyes." Instead, focus on details that genuinely appeal to you, such as "I love your vintage leather jacket - it suits you perfectly" or "Your presentation today was incredibly informative; I learned so much!" Specific praises convey honesty and make a stronger impact than general statements. Step 3: Deliver the Compliment with Confidence Key Tip: Maintain eye contact, smile warmly, and speak clearly to express positivity and certainty. When delivering the compliment, adopt a confident posture and maintain eye contact to establish a connection. Speak audibly, using a friendly tone to ensure your words are heard and understood. Accompany your verbal praise with a genuine smile, conveying enthusiasm and approval without appearing insincere or overbearing. A confident approach encourages open communication and sets a comfortable atmosphere for further interaction. Step 4: Be Sincere and Genuine Key Guideline: Mean what you say, avoiding exaggerated flattery or manipulative tactics. Sincerity is crucial when giving compliments. Exaggerating or falsifying praises may lead to mistrust and damage relationships. Stick to genuine observations and avoid excessive flattery, which can come across as disingenuous. Remember that the goal of complementing is to create a positive experience for both parties involved, fostering mutual understanding and appreciation. Step 5: Respect Boundaries Key Tip: Recognize social cues and respond appropriately to maintain comfort and dignity. While paying compliments, always remain aware of social boundaries and cultural norms. If the man appears uncomfortable or uninterested in continuing the conversation, gracefully excuse yourself to prevent awkwardness. Furthermore, refrain from making invasive or objectifying remarks regarding physical appearance or personal life. Maintaining respect ensures a pleasant encounter for everyone involved. Conclusion: Giving compliments to a man who catches your eye can be an enjoyable and rewarding experience for both parties. By observing attentively, crafting personalized praises, delivering them confidently, being sincere, and respecting boundaries, you can effectively communicate admiration while fostering meaningful connections. With practice, these steps become second nature, enhancing interpersonal skills and promoting overall wellbeing. Do I height eat all of this? And all the way up to the bottom. " [as Lin picks up a small cup from the table] [attempts to place blue checkered square magnet and yellow triangle magnet on black base; both fall off] And he was trying to catch it. Hunt for Words In this word game, children have to find small words inside of long words.Materials - One pencil per player - One sheet of paper per player Write a long word like concentration or spaghetti at the top of each child's paper. At your signal, have the children write as many two-, three-, and four-letter words as they can using only the letters that appear in the long word you've chosen. (Let the children use proper nouns, plurals, slang, acronyms, and so on.) Stop the game after five minutes. The child who has written the most words is the winner. A child playing alone can simply race the clock and try to beat her previous record. More on: Activities for Kids Copyright 2001 by Patricia Kuffner. Eating wheat germ can be a simple and easy way to add more nutrients to your diet. Here is a detailed guide on how to eat wheat germ, with in-depth explanations for each step: **Step 1: Understand What Wheat Germ Is** ----------------------------------------- Wheat germ is the part of the wheat kernel that contains the most nutrients. It is often removed during the milling process when making white flour because it has a short shelf life and can go rancid quickly due to its high oil content. However, this also means that it is packed full of vitamins, minerals, and other beneficial compounds. **Step 2: Choose Your Wheat Germ** ---------------------------------- There are two main types of wheat germ: toasted and untoasted. Untoasted wheat germ has a milder flavor and can be eaten raw or cooked, while toasted wheat germ has a nuttier taste and is usually used as a topping or ingredient in baked goods. Both types provide similar nutritional benefits. When purchasing wheat germ, look for products that have been refrigerated or frozen, as this will help preserve their freshness and nutrient content. You can find wheat germ at most grocery stores, either in the baking aisle or near the breakfast cereals. **Step 3: Decide How to Consume Wheat Germ** ----------------------------------------------- There are many ways to incorporate wheat germ into your diet. Some popular methods include: * Sprinkling it on top of yogurt, oatmeal, or fruit salad for added crunch and nutrition * Mixing it into smoothies or protein shakes * Adding it to bread, muffin, pancake, or waffle batter * Using it as a coating for chicken or fish before baking or frying * Stirring it into pasta sauce, casseroles, or soups for an extra boost of fiber and nutrients **Step 4: Measure Appropriate Serving Sizes** --------------------------------------------- A typical serving size of wheat germ is about 2 tablespoons (16 grams), which provides around 50 calories, 4 grams of carbohydrates, 2 grams of fiber, and 3 grams of protein. This serving size also offers various essential vitamins and minerals like Vitamin E, folate, magnesium, phosphorus, thiamine, zinc, and iron. Keep track of your daily intake to ensure you're consuming an appropriate amount based on your personal health goals and needs. **Step 5: Store Wheat Germ Properly** ------------------------------------- To maintain optimal freshness and prevent spoilage, store your wheat germ in an airtight container in the refrigerator or freezer. When stored properly, it should last up to six months in the fridge and up to one year in the freezer. Regularly checking for signs of rancidity, such as off odors or changes in color, will help guarantee that your wheat germ remains safe and enjoyable to consume. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Gradually introduce wheat germ into your diet if you haven't consumed it before, as too much at once may cause digestive discomfort. * Always consult with a healthcare professional before adding any new supplements or food sources to your diet, especially if you have specific medical concerns or allergies. * Be aware that wheat germ does contain gluten, so individuals with celiac disease or non-celiac gluten sensitivity should avoid eating it. [using her hands to emphasize what she is saying] |Marine Life||page 1| Not all animals glide or fly in the air. Many marine (water) animals are masters of "flight" and speed under the water. Ocean, or water, environments cause these animals to adapt (change to fit that environment). Underwater Dynamics (bodies in motion) Most fish are too heavy to float. Some sink to the ocean floor and live there. Other fish have a built-in swim-bladder (like a small gas filled ballon) inside. This helps them stay off the ground and up in the water. Still others, like sharks, must swim all the time to stay up, because they don't have a swim-bladder. It is difficult to swim through water. Water is thicker than air. Fast fish have a fusiform body (shaped like a torpedo or rocket). Some fish are flat on both sides (laterally compressed). They can easily swim around coral reefs. The fish living on the bottom of the ocean are flat - both top and bottom. This helps them glide along the bottom. Fast moving fish have special features. Besides being shaped like a torpedo they can pull in fins not in use to reduce drag. They may also have a slimy mucous layer (like oil) that helps them glide through the water. Or they may have special skin control to reduce drag. Fins are found in many places on a fish's body. There are side fins, dorsal (top and bottom) and caudel (tail) fins. These fins move the fish forward, help with turns and keeping balance. The tail fin pushes the fish forward. Broad tail fins give a fast start. Long narrow tails are better for long distance swimming. When most fish swim, they undulate (bend) their bodies in an S-shaped wave. This wave motion begins at the head and travels down the body toward the tail. This pushes (propels) the fish forward. Fish use their muscles to move their bodies and fins. The larger white muscles are for speed, but they tire easily. The red muscles are for slow or regular movements. The fish can swim a long time with the red muscles. If the muscles need more oxygen the fish can open its mouth to let more water pass over its gills. The gills take oxygen from the water and give it to the muscles. Cetaceans (Dolphins and Whales) Dolphins and whales, like fish, spend their whole lives in the water. But, dolphins and whales are mammals. This means they are warm-blooded, have live births and nurse their young. It also means they must breathe air through their nose (blowhole). Dolphins and whales swim differently than fish. A fish's tail moves from side to side. A dolphin's tail moves up and down. The dolphin's tail is so powerful it can push the dolphin straight up out of the water. Dolphins often push themselves out of the water (jump). The reason they do this is to take a breath of air. That way the dolphin does not need to slow down in the water. Flying fish do not really "fly". They glide across the surface of the water. They can glide very fast and very far through the air. As the fish leaves the water special fins are spread out that allows it to glide. Rays and skates live on the bottom of the ocean (they have no swim bladder). They use their fins in a different way than fish. Fish move their fins sideways. The ray moves its fins in an S-shaped wave, up and down, from the head to the tail. Some water animals have flippers. Flippers can act like an airplane wing in the water. The sea turtle, sea lion and seal are examples of animals with flipppers. The movement of the flippers allows the animal to go up or down or forward in the water. Sea turtles and sea lions use their front flippers to move forward. Seals use their tail flippers to move forward. Several shelled animals such as scallops and clams also use jet propulsion to get away from enemies. To do this the animal must bring together its two shells, which forces water out sending it to safety. Click on the picture to see the clam move! Were there any specific ones that you found she played with now and not last time? = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Gleason/Mother/Eddie.cha = = = How to Tackle Word Problems on the ACT A word problem (also called a story problem or a problem in a setting) gives you information in words rather than in just equations and numbers. To answer an ACT word problem, you have to translate the provided information into one or more equations and then solve. You can solve some word problems fairly easily. Jotting down the numbers in the problem can be useful to help get you focused and moving in the right direction. The following example word problems show you how. Seminar X brought in $700 in revenue and had 20 participants, each of whom paid the same amount. Seminar Y brought in $750 and had 15 participants, each of whom paid the same amount. How much more did each person pay for Seminar Y than Seminar X? (E) The two seminars cost the same amount. If you're not immediately sure how to proceed, jot down the numbers in an orderly fashion: This step only takes a moment and gets your brain moving. When you organize the information in this way, you may see that the next step involves division: Now you can easily see that Seminar Y cost $15 more than Seminar X, so the correct answer is Choice (C). Jessica is in charge of stocking shelves at a supermarket. Today, she has already stocked 8 boxes that each contained 40 cans of soup, 12 boxes that each contained 24 cans of corned beef hash, and 4 boxes that each contained 60 cans of tuna. How many cans has Jessica stocked today? Record the numbers in this question as follows: Now multiply these numbers across (with or without your calculator, as needed) to get the number of cans in each set of boxes: Finish by adding the results: 320 + 288 + 240 = 848. Therefore, the correct answer is Choice (J). Some word problems are much easier to solve when you draw a sketch to organize your thoughts. This technique is especially helpful if you're a visual learner. So if you like to draw, paint, or play video games, lead with your strength and try to find a visual way to express math problems whenever possible. The following example shows how to use a sketch to your advantage. The 12:00 p.m. eastbound train left the station at a constant speed of 40 miles per hour. At 12:45 p.m., the next eastbound train left the station at a constant speed of 60 miles per hour. Assuming neither train stops along the way, how far apart will the two trains be at 2:00 p.m.? (A) 5 miles (B) 10 miles (C) 12 miles (D) 15 miles (E) 18 miles This problem is difficult to visualize, so sketching out the information can help you arrive at the correct answer: This figure helps illustrate a way into the problem: By 2:00 p.m., the 12:00 p.m. train has traveled for 2 hours at 40 miles per hour, so it's 80 miles from the station. And the 12:45 p.m. train has traveled for 1 hour and 15 minutes at 60 miles per hour. The 1 hour accounts for 60 miles. The 15 minutes is a quarter of an hour, so this accounts for 15 miles. Thus, the 12:45 p.m. train is 75 miles from the station. As a result, the trains are 5 miles apart, making the correct answer Choice (A). T h r o u g h t. Um probably not fun to chew on I would say. Juicy Stuff Matters Reading to Learn Summarization is an important skill for any reader to learn and master a review or recap can take place. Summarization is an important literacy goal because it helps students to understand been read and gives them the necessary scaffolding to represent their of the material read. This lesson will teach students story structures, learn to summarize by singling out what is important, and how to sue summarization on sentence strips to practice putting a story - Dr. Seuss. How the Grinch Stole Christmas. 1957. New York, Random House. - Paper and pencil - Sentence strips - I will introduce summarization to the students by asking them what it means and then describing the concept in my own words. "What does it mean to summarize a story? Summarizing a story means to read a passage and then retell the story by using the main idea so that it is shorter. In order to do this we must be able to pick out what is important to the plot of the story. This helps us understand what happened in the story in a quick and organized way. I will model for you by reading a passage and summarizing it. (I wanted a puppy for Christmas. I ask my mom for a puppy everyday. I knew Christmas was going to be here soon so I even asked her three times a day so that she would not forget. When we walked past the pet store I would point out the puppy I wanted so that my mom wouldn't pick the wrong one. I even wrote Santa Clause and asked him for a puppy just in case my mom forgot. If she didn't forget, I would get two puppies. When Christmas Day came I ran down stairs to see all the presents piled under the tree. I looked for the box that would be moving and making noises, but I couldn't find one. After we opened all our presents, I still was not as happy as I should have been. I asked my mom if she forgot about the puppy I wanted. As I was waiting for her answer, my dad walked through the door with the puppy I wanted? He didn't forget about my puppy!) Summarization: I asked my mom and Santa Clause everyday for a puppy for Christmas and when Christmas Day came, my dad was the one who remembered to get my puppy. "I just summarized the story by going through each sentence and finding information that is important to know and will help me understand the book. To find out what is important you should ask yourself these questions: Who is in the story? Where are the characters? What did the characters do? What was the problem in the story and was the problem solved? Use these questions to help you find out what is important. Not everyone will have the same answer and that is ok. - Explain to the students that they will be put into small groups and must work cooperatively. The students will be put into groups of three. Each group will get a copy of the book, How the Grinch Stole Christmas. They will be assigned a certain number of pages to read so that the text is brief. "A good way to summarize is to write notes of what is important. (I will read the first page of the book and write on a post-it what is important on that page so that you know exactly how to do it.) If you don't think important information is on that page, then don't write anything on your post-it. You can stick the post-it on the page you read so that you can go back to see if what you wrote is really important. I will model how to use the post-its. (I will read the first two pages of the story out loud and as I'm reading, I will stop and say what I'm doing) Page one: "Every Who down in Who-Ville liked Christmas a lot" I will take my post-it and ask myself the questions stated above and write the answers down. If I don't have an answer, I won't write anything down. I will explain this out loud to the students. I write on the post-it "Who", "Who-Ville", and "they like Christmas a lot". I will explain why I chose to write the words that were in the story. "I must know who is in the story, where the story is taking place, and what the characters are doing or what they like." I will repeat this process for the second page. - The children will take turns reading pages in their groups and write their post-it notes. After reading their assigned pages, students should discuss as a group what they read and decide if the notes answer the questions. - The groups will then rewrite their notes in sentences and write them on sentence strips. - The class will come together and each group will put their sentence strips in order on the board. The class will decide if they are truly in order and if they think this is a good summarization of the story. I will ask, "Do you think that this summarization can be shortened any more without taking out the important information? If so, what can you say?" I will assess students while they are in their groups and as they are on their post-its, I will ask them why they chose those ideas and why think they are important. Getting Down to the Nitty Gritty. Ireland. The most carefully reasoned argument covering all possible cases was done by Xin Ying from Tao Nan School, Singapore. Let R, G, Y and B be the number of Red, Green, Yellow and Blue There are 4 conditions to fulfil: (1) R + G + Y + B = 12; (2) R > G; (3) (G+B) > R; (4) (Y+G)> (R+B). We shall use logical thinking to get the From condition (3), (R+B) is at most (12 2) - 1 = So (R+B) could be 5, 4, 3, 2, or 1. Case 1 : (R+B)=1 Impossible as R and B are both not zeros. Case 2 : R+B = 2 Then, R=1 and B=1. But R> G and G is not zero. So this case is Case 3 : (R+B) = 3 Then (R+B) = 1+2 or 2 +1. But it must be that R=2 and B=1 so as to fulfil condition (2). Now, R> G tells us that G=1. But (G+B) is not greater than R. Condition (3) is violated. So this case is Case 4 : (R+B) = 4 (a) R+B = 1+3 or 3+1. It must be that R=3 and B=1 from condition (2). Since R> G, G=2 or 1. Either case, (G+B) is not greater than R. So this case is impossible. (b) R+B=2+2. Then R=2 and B=2. Since R> G, G=1. Then, Y=7. Upon checking, all 4 conditions are fulfilled. This case works! Case 5 : (R+B)=5 (a) R=2 and B=3. Since R> G, G=1. So, Y=6. Upon checking, all 4 conditions are fulfilled. This case works! (b) R=3 and B=2. Since R> G, G=2 or 1. If G=1, (G+B) is not greater than R. This case is not possible. So G=2. Then Y=5. Upon checking, all 4 conditions are fulfilled. This case works! (c) R + B = 4 + 1 (1+4 is impossible). So R=4 and B=1. Since R> G, G = 3, 2 or 1. But either case, (G+B) is not greater than R. This case is impossible. In short, only 3 situations are possible: 5 yellow marbles, 3 red marbles, 2 blue marbles and 2 green 7 yellow marbles, 2 red marbles, 2 blue marbles and 1 green 6 yellow, 2 red, 3 blue and 1 green marbles. Title: Act Like a Furby: A Comprehensive Guide Furbies are beloved electronic toys that have captured hearts since their introduction in 1998. They communicate using a mix of squeaks, chirps, and gibberish words, which has inspired some people to mimic these sounds and behaviors. This guide will teach you how to act like a Furby with precision and enthusiasm! **Step 1: Understand Your Audience** Before diving into acting like a Furby, consider your audience. Will they appreciate this behavior? If so, proceed; if not, reconsider or save it for an appropriate time. *Key tip:* Authenticity is crucial. Embrace the playful spirit of Furbies rather than mocking them. **Step 2: Study Furby Sounds** Listen to various Furby noises to familiarize yourself with their unique language. Watch videos online or interact with real Furbies if possible. Pay attention to pitch, tone, speed, and volume. *Guideline:* Practice makes perfect - listen multiple times until you're comfortable reproducing the sounds. **Step 3: Master Basic Vocalizations** Start by learning basic vocalizations such as greetings ("Furby here!"), responses ("Me furby!"), questions ("What dat?"), and emotions (happy, sad, surprised). These form the foundation of your Furby persona. *Key tip:* Focus on inflection and intonation. High-pitched tones convey excitement while lower ones express curiosity or confusion. **Step 4: Learn Complex Phrases** As you progress, move onto more complex phrases like "My bellie feel funny" or "I wanna dance." Remember, pronunciation may differ from standard English due to Furby's distinct speech patterns. *Guideline:* Repeat after listening carefully, focusing on individual syllables and overall rhythm. **Step 5: Imitate Physical Movements** Observe how Furbies physically react when speaking. Common movements include tilting heads, blinking eyes rapidly, wagging tails, and twitching ears. Try replicating these gestures during conversation. *Key tip:* Use body language sparingly at first, gradually incorporating more movement as you become more confident. **Step 6: Develop Responses** Anticipate potential interactions by preparing answers to common prompts. For example, if someone asks "Do you want food?," respond enthusiastically with "Yummy nommy!" *Guideline:* Prepare several stock phrases for different situations, ensuring variety and unpredictability. **Step 7: Engage in Conversation** Initiate conversations by asking open-ended questions like "You doin' stuff?" or making statements such as "Furby see pretty colors." Encourage others to participate through active listening and genuine interest. *Key tip:* Maintain eye contact and nod along, signaling engagement and understanding. **Step 8: Adapt Accordingly** Be prepared to adjust based on feedback. Some people might find your impersonation endearing, while others could be confused or annoyed. Always prioritize respect and kindness towards others. *Guideline:* Regularly assess whether your performance adds positivity and amusement without causing discomfort. By following these steps, you'll soon master the art of acting like a Furby. Enjoy bringing joy and laughter to those around you through this whimsical and entertaining impression! If it does not dissolve, it would be called a suspension. Title: How to Have Samurott in Pok mon White Pok mon White is a popular role-playing game developed by Game Freak and published by Nintendo. One of the fascinating features of this game is collecting and training various Pok mons. This tutorial will guide you through the process of obtaining and evolving a Samurott, the final evolution form of the starting Pok mon, Oshawott, in Pok mon White. **Step 1: Choose Oshawott as Your Starter Pok mon** When you start playing Pok mon White, Professor Juniper will offer you a choice between three starter Pok mons - Tepig, Snivy, and Oshawott. Select Oshawott to begin your journey towards having a Samurott. Choosing Oshawott provides you with a direct path to achieving your goal since it's the initial stage of Samurott's evolution line. **Step 2: Level Up Oshawott to Evolve It into Dewott** To evolve Oshawott into its next form, Dewott, you need to gain experience points (XP) through battles until it reaches level 17. You can battle wild Pok mons or trainers' Pok mons to earn XP. Once Oshawott gains enough XP and hits level 17, it will automatically evolve into Dewott during the battle or when switching out afterwards. Key Tips: * Engage in battles frequently to ensure steady growth in levels. * Use items like Potions and Antidotes to keep your Oshawott healthy during fights. * If possible, try to avoid using Oshawott in tough battles where there's a high chance of defeat; instead, consider using other stronger team members. **Step 3: Level Up Dewott to Evolve It into Samurott** Just like before, continue battling and gaining XP to reach level 34, at which point Dewott will evolve into Samurott. Again, this happens either mid-battle or post-battle after being switched out. Key Guidelines: * Be strategic about which opponents you choose to fight against. Ideally, they should be weak compared to your current party so that your Dewott doesn't get knocked out too often. * Make sure to heal your Pok mon regularly using Centers found throughout Unova region. **Step 4: Utilize Samurott Effectively** Samurott is a Water-type Pok mon known for its power and defensive capabilities. Its move set includes powerful attacks such as Hydro Pump, Aqua Jet, Razor Shell, and Megahorn. As a well-rounded fighter, Samurott can serve many roles within your team depending on what moves you teach it. Remember, capturing and evolving Pok mons involves strategy and patience. Kami-shibai (Japanese: ) is a storytelling performance with picture cards, and is a traditional Japanese entertainment for children. In Japanese "Kami" means paper and "shibai" means drama or enternainment. The performer tells a story and shows pictures from scene to scene. Kami-shibai is a bidirectional media which is comprised of actions and reactions between a performer and audiences. With the spread of television, kami-shibai went out of date. See they put the water in their trunks there? Light waves have "crests" and "troughs". The highest point of a wave is the crest, and the lowest part of the wave is a trough. Sometimes more than one of these waves can interfere with each other - the crests and the troughs line up. This is called "light interference".
