Title: How to Choose Makeup for Bridal Photography 1. Consult with the Bride: The first step in choosing makeup for bridal photography is to consult with the bride. Discuss her preferences, skin type, and any specific concerns she may have. This will help you create a look that she feels comfortable and confident in. It's important to remember that this is her special day, and the makeup should reflect her personal style and enhance her natural beauty. 2. Understand the Lighting: The lighting used during the photography session can significantly affect the appearance of the makeup. Natural light tends to wash out colors, while artificial light can bring them out. Therefore, it's crucial to choose shades that complement the expected lighting conditions. For example, soft, neutral tones work well in natural light, while bolder colors can be used in artificial light. 3. Prep the Skin: Properly preparing the skin before applying makeup can make a significant difference in the final result. Start by cleansing and moisturizing the face to create a smooth canvas. Then, apply a primer to help the makeup last longer and prevent it from looking cakey under the camera flash. 4. Create a Flawless Base: Achieving a flawless base is essential in bridal makeup as it forms the foundation for the rest of the look. Use a medium-to-full coverage foundation that matches the bride's skin tone perfectly. Conceal any imperfections, but avoid overdoing it as heavy concealer can appear unnatural in photographs. Set the base with a translucent powder to minimize shine and ensure longevity. 5. Enhance Facial Features: Subtly enhancing the bride's facial features can help her stand out in photos. Use a matte bronzer to contour the cheeks, jawline, and temples, creating definition and warmth. Highlight the high points of the face (cheekbones, brow bone, cupid's bow) with a champagne or gold highlight to add dimension and glow. Well it's pink, and it's called a pitcher with a spout on. Title: How to Have a Successful Vegetarian Barbecue ================================================= A vegetarian barbecue can be just as delicious and satisfying as a traditional meat-based one. With careful planning and execution, you can create a memorable outdoor dining experience that caters to all dietary preferences. Here is an extensive guide on how to have a successful vegetarian barbecue, complete with in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines. Step 1: Plan Your Guest List ---------------------------- Before preparing your menu or purchasing supplies, determine who will attend your event. This information allows you to consider any specific dietary needs and plan accordingly. When inviting guests, mention that the gathering is vegetarian so they are aware beforehand and can prepare themselves if necessary. ### Why this Step Matters Planning your guest list ensures that everyone feels welcome at your event. It also prevents last-minute surprises when people show up expecting meat dishes but only find plant-based options available. By informing attendees about the nature of the barbecue ahead of time, you allow them to adjust their expectations or bring additional items if needed. Step 2: Create a Menu --------------------- Design a diverse menu featuring various vegetables, grains, legumes, fruits, cheeses, and other non-meat ingredients. Offer both cold salads and hot dishes cooked directly on the grill. Keep in mind different textures, flavors, and nutritional profiles while planning. Additionally, consider offering vegan alternatives for those avoiding dairy products or eggs. #### Suggested Dishes: * Grilled portobello mushrooms * Marinated tofu steaks * Stuffed bell peppers * Corn on the cob * Eggplant rolls filled with ricotta cheese and herbs * Zucchini boats stuffed with quinoa and black beans * Roasted sweet potato wedges * Grilled pineapple slices * Veggie burgers and sausages * Greek salad with feta cheese * Quinoa tabbouleh * Three bean salad * Caprese skewers (mozzarella, tomatoes, basil) * Grilled paneer tikka ![Vegetarian BBQ Menu Ideas]( [Linda takes the whistle away. If you're new to crocheting, consider practicing these stitches first. **Step 1: Chain Four** Chain four to create your foundation chain. This will serve as the base row for your hand warmer. The number of chains may vary depending on your preferred size; adjust accordingly but always ensure it's an even number. **Step 2: Double Crochet Sixteen** In the second row, perform double crochet stitches into the fourth chain from the hook and every subsequent chain. You should have 16 dc stitches when completed. This creates the bottom edge of your hand warmer. **Step 3: Increase Rows** To make the hand warmer fitted, we need to increase the width gradually. For rows three through seven, work one increase every other row like so: Row 3 - sc in each dc across, Row 5 - hdc in each stitch across, then repeat steps 3 & 5 another two times. An increase means adding an extra stitch between two existing ones. It could be done by making 2 dc or hdc in the same stitch or creating a space and working into it. **Step 4: Decrease Rows** After reaching the desired width at the top part of the hand warmer, we now decrease to shape the thumb area. Starting with Row 13, decreease as follows: *skip next stitch, dc in next stitch*; rep from \*. Do this until there are only two stitches left, then finish off with a sl st to join. Repeat this process on the opposite side of the piece. **Step 5: Creating Thumb Opening** Identify where your thumb would naturally fit within the decreased section. Mark this spot with a stitch marker if needed. Unravel the decreases made earlier up till this point. Instead of skipping stitches, continue with normal dc or hdc stitches until you reach the marked position. At the marked position, create eight chain stitches which act as a temporary placeholder for the thumb hole. Then continue decreasing normally till end of row. Remember to mirror this pattern on the opposite side too. **Step 6: Finalizing Thumb Hole** Once both sides are complete including the thumb holes, connect them using slip stitch. Weave in all ends using a tapestry needle. Try it on to check fitting. If necessary, block gently using warm water and let dry before wearing. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Always count your stitches after completing each row to ensure accuracy. - Use a larger hook for looser, more comfortable hand warmers; use a smaller hook for denser, warmer hand warmers. - Experiment with different types of yarns for varied textures and appearances. - Adjust the length, width, and thumb placement according to individual hand sizes. - When increasing or decreasing, remember to distribute them evenly throughout the row for balanced shaping. [looks at MOT, then in the container] Title: How to Attend a Medical Appointment on Zoom Introduction: With the advent of technology, medical appointments have become more accessible than ever before. One such platform that enables remote consultations is Zoom. This tutorial will guide you through attending a medical appointment on Zoom with clear, concise steps and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Schedule Your Appointment Consult your healthcare provider about scheduling a telemedicine visit via Zoom. They'll provide you with a meeting link or ask you to download the app if necessary. Make sure they send you any required forms or questionnaires ahead of time so you can fill them out prior to the appointment. Key Tip: Be aware of your local laws regarding virtual medical visits as some states may require specific regulations be followed. Step 2: Prepare Your Space Choose a quiet, well-lit area free from distractions. Ideally, this should be a private room where you can discuss sensitive information without interruption. If possible, avoid noisy environments like coffee shops or public areas during video calls. Guideline: Test your internet connection beforehand to ensure there are no technical difficulties during the actual consultation. Step 3: Set Up Equipment Use a computer or laptop rather than a mobile device when feasible since larger screens make reading text easier. Position yourself so that the camera captures your head and shoulders; this allows the doctor to see nonverbal cues. Also, check that your microphone and speakers work correctly. Helpful Hint: Consider using headphones with built-in microphones for better audio quality. Step 4: Install Necessary Software Download Zoom (if not already installed) and sign up for an account. You do not need a paid plan for basic video conferencing features. Once downloaded, familiarize yourself with its interface by clicking around different options. Important Note: Always keep your software updated to benefit from security patches and improvements. Step 5: Join The Meeting At the appointed hour, click on the provided Zoom link. Enter the password given by your health care provider when prompted. Remember, these details might change every session depending on their preferences. Caution: Never share your personal meeting ID or URL publicly, even though it seems convenient. It could expose you to unwanted participants ("Zoombombers"). Step 6: Follow Professional Etiquette Dress appropriately just as you would for an in-person visit. During the call, maintain eye contact whenever possible and limit background noise. Try to remain seated unless getting up demonstrates something relevant to your condition. Did You Know? Virtual whiteboards exist within Zoom which doctors can utilize to illustrate concepts related to your health. Step 7: Take Notes Jot down important points discussed during the appointment. These notes serve as reminders after the session ends. Afterward, review what was covered and write down any questions you forgot to ask earlier. Remember: Don't hesitate to request clarification on anything unclear during the conversation. Conclusion: By following these simple yet effective steps, you're now ready to attend your medical appointment virtually via Zoom! Telemedicine offers numerous benefits including convenience, reduced travel time, and potentially lower costs. However, remember that while technology simplifies things, human interaction remains crucial - smile, engage, and build rapport with your physician despite being miles apart. Compressing web content using Gzip compression is an effective way to reduce bandwidth usage and improve website load times. This tutorial will guide you through the process of implementing Gzip compression on your website, with clear explanations for each step and best practices to ensure optimal results. ### Step 1: Understand Gzip Compression Gzip is a lossless data compression algorithm that can significantly reduce the size of HTML, CSS, JavaScript, XML, and other text-based files by finding and removing repetitive patterns within the file. When enabled on a server, Gzip compresses these files before sending them to the client's browser, which then decompresses the files for rendering the webpage. As compressed files are smaller, they require less bandwidth and download faster, providing a better user experience. Key Tips: * Gzip does not modify or degrade image quality as it only works with text-based files. * Implementing Gzip compression can result in up to 70% reduction in file sizes, depending on the type and structure of the content. ### Step 2: Check if Your Website Already Uses Gzip Compression Before enabling Gzip compression, check whether your site already uses it. There are several online tools available that can help you determine this, such as Google PageSpeed Insights, GTmetrix, or WebPageTest. Enter your website URL into one of these tools, run the test, and look for any mention of 'gzip' or 'compressed' under the performance or optimization sections. If Gzip compression is already implemented, no further action is required. ### Step 3: Enable Gzip Compression (Apache) If your website runs on Apache, you can enable Gzip compression by adding the following code snippet to your .htaccess file, usually located at the root directory of your public_html folder: ```python <ifModule mod_deflate.c> # Title: Fight Fatigue While Pregnant Pregnancy is an exciting time, but it can also be exhausting. As your body works hard to support your growing baby, you may experience fatigue that goes beyond normal tiredness. This tutorial will provide you with steps and tips to help combat pregnancy-related fatigue. ### Step 1: Understand Why You're Feeling Tired During early pregnancy, high levels of progesterone cause sleepiness. Later in pregnancy, your expanding uterus puts pressure on your organs, making you uncomfortable and causing insomnia. Moreover, increased blood volume and metabolism require more energy from your body, leading to fatigue. Recognizing these causes can help you manage expectations and take appropriate action. ### Step 2: Prioritize Sleep Sleep becomes even more crucial during pregnancy. Aim for at least eight hours of sleep per night. If possible, nap during the day when you feel exhausted. Early bedtime might become necessary as well. Don't fight it; listen to what your body needs. Key Tip: Make sure your mattress provides adequate support and comfort. Consider investing in a comfortable pregnancy pillow to alleviate discomfort caused by your changing body shape. ### Step 3: Eat Nutritious Foods Eating small, frequent meals throughout the day keeps your blood sugar stable, reducing feelings of lethargy. Focus on nutrient-dense foods like fruits, vegetables, lean proteins, whole grains, and dairy products. These foods give you sustained energy without causing spikes and crashes associated with sugary snacks. Guideline: Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water. Dehydration can lead to fatigue and other health issues. However, avoid consuming too much liquid before bedtime to minimize nighttime trips to the bathroom. ### Step 4: Exercise Regularly Exercise releases endorphins which boost mood and increase energy levels. Engage in low-impact exercises such as walking, swimming, or prenatal yoga. Always consult your healthcare provider about safe exercise routines during pregnancy. Key Tip: Avoid strenuous activities close to bedtime because they could interfere with your ability to fall asleep. ### Step 5: Manage Stress Levels High stress levels drain energy quickly. Practice relaxation techniques like deep breathing, meditation, or guided imagery to reduce anxiety and tension. Joining a childbirth education class or talking to a counselor specializing in maternal mental health are additional options. Guideline: Establish a calming pre-sleep routine to signal your brain that it's time to wind down. Activities like reading a book (not on a screen), taking a warm bath, or listening to soothing music can promote better quality sleep. ### Step 6: Seek Support When Needed Don't hesitate to ask for help if daily tasks seem overwhelming. Whether it's housework, grocery shopping, or caring for existing children, accepting assistance allows you to rest when needed. Key Tip: Consider joining a local mothers' group or online forum where you can share experiences and advice with others going through similar situations. Remember, every woman's pregnancy journey is unique, and some degree of fatigue is perfectly normal. By following these steps, however, you can mitigate excessive tiredness and enjoy this special time in your life. Consult your healthcare professional if persistent fatigue remains unmanageable or accompanies symptoms like severe headaches, vision changes, chest pain, or bleeding. Bunny Cakes by Rosemary Wells Summary of the Story Max makes an earthworm cake for Grandma's birthday and wants to help his sister Ruby make her angel surprise cake for Grandma. However, Max's "help" results in many spilled ingredients. Ruby sends Max to the store for replacements. At the store, the grocer can't read all the items on the shopping list until Max solves the problem by drawing pictures.Introducing the Story - Read the title of the book on the cover, pointing to each word as you say it. Have children repeat the title as you point to each word. - Ask children to look at the pictures and think about what the story will be about. Ask: What do you think the bunnies are going to make? (They are going to make cakes.) Reading the Story for the First Time - Read the story, moving your finger under the words as you read. - After reading, say: The bunnies in this book make cakes. When do we like to eat cake? (We like to eat cake on birthdays and other special occasions.) Recalling the Story - After you have finished reading, ask children the recall questions below. Continue to ask these questions when you reread the book, until he or she knows the answers. Reading the Story Again and Again - Give open-ended prompts on each page. For example, ask: What's happening on this page? Do less reading of the words to the story each time you read, leaving more and more of the "reading" or retelling to the child. - Give prompts about objects or activities in the pictures. For example, ask: What is Max using to make his earthworm cake? (He is using a pancake turner.) Use your finger to point to what you are asking about. Evaluate the children's response. Expand it by adding more information. Ask the child to repeat the answer. If he or she needs help in answering a question, ask that question again the next time you read the book. - You may wish to discuss the prompts shown below. - Have children read Bunny Cakes to each other. - Children can dramatize the story using play food. Assign parts for Max, Ruby, and the grocer. They can act out the story while you read it aloud. Ask the following questions to check the child's understanding of the story. - What is the title of this book? (Bunny Cakes) - What kind of cake does Max make? (He makes an earthworm cake.) - What kind of cake does Ruby want to make? (She wants to make an angel surprise cake with raspberry-fluff icing.) - What happens when Max tries to help Ruby? (Max breaks the eggs and spills the milk and the flour.) - Where does Ruby send Max to get the eggs, milk, and flour? (She sends him to the grocery store.) - How does the grocer know what Ruby needs? (He reads what Ruby has written on the list.) - Why doesn't Max get what he wants at the grocery store? (He wants Red-Hot Marshmallow Squirters for his cake, but the grocer can't read his scribbles and so doesn't give him what he wants.) - How does Max finally get the Red-Hot Marshmallow Squirters for his cake? (He draws a picture of them, and the grocer then knows exactly what he wants.) More on: Skill-Building Activities Sometime you'll have a funny thought about somethin'. Sara was a smart girl who liked to read books and learn new things. She had many books in her room, but she wanted more. She asked her mom and dad to buy her more books, but they said no. They said she had enough books and she should share them with her brother and sister. Sara was not happy. She did not want to share her books. She thought her brother and sister were not smart enough to read them. She sighed and went to her room. She locked the door and started to read a book about the weather. She liked to learn about the sun, the rain, the wind and the snow. But soon she heard a knock on the door. It was her brother. He said he wanted to read the book about the weather with her. He said he liked to learn about the sun, the rain, the wind and the snow too. Sara said no. She said the book was hers and he could not read it. She told him to go away. Her brother was sad. He sighed and went to his room. He did not have many books, but he had a toy car. He liked to play with his toy car and make it go fast. He thought maybe Sara would like to play with his toy car too. He went to her door again and knocked. He said he wanted to share his toy car with her. He said they could play together and have fun. Sara said no. She said she did not like toy cars. She said they were boring and silly. She told him to go away again. Her brother was very sad. He sighed and went to his room. He did not want to play with his toy car alone. He wished Sara would be nice to him and share her books. But Sara did not care. She kept reading her book about the weather. She thought she was very smart and did not need anyone else. But then something happened. The weather changed. It started to rain very hard. The rain made a loud noise on the roof and the windows. Sara was scared. She did not like the rain. She wanted it to stop. She wanted to see the sun again. She ran to her mom and dad. She said she was afraid of the rain. She asked them to make it stop. They said they could not make it stop. They said the rain was part of the weather and they had to wait for it to pass. They said they could read a book or play a game to pass the time. Sara said she did not want to read a book or play a game. She said she wanted to see the sun. She said she hated the rain. She said she hated the weather. Her mom and dad looked at her. They said she was not being smart. They said she was being selfish and rude. They said she should be grateful for the books she had and the weather she had. They said she should be kind to her brother and sister and share with them. They said they loved her, but they were disappointed in her. Sara felt bad. She realized she had been wrong. She realized she had hurt her brother and sister. She realized she had been ungrateful and foolish. She sighed and said she was sorry. She said she wanted to be smart and nice. She said she wanted to share her books and play with her brother and sister. She said she loved them and the weather. Her mom and dad smiled. They said they were proud of her. They said they forgave her. They said they loved her too. They hugged her and said they would read a book and play a game with her and her brother and sister. Sara was happy. She ran to her room and opened the door. She saw her brother and sister waiting for her. They had brought their books and toys. Applying for a marriage license in Tennessee is a straightforward process that can be completed by following these steps: **Step 1: Gather Necessary Information and Documents** Before visiting the county clerk's office, gather the necessary information and documents. This includes: * Proof of age (driver's license, passport, or state-issued ID) * Social security number (social security card or another document showing your SSN) * If either party has been previously married, proof of the termination of the most recent marriage (divorce decree, death certificate, etc.) must be presented. Having this information ready will help streamline the application process and avoid any delays. **Step 2: Visit the County Clerk's Office** In Tennessee, you can apply for a marriage license at any county clerk's office, regardless of where in the state you plan to marry. The couple must appear together when applying for the license. It is important to note that some counties may require an appointment, so it is recommended to check ahead of time. This step brings you closer to obtaining a legal authorization to get married in Tennessee. By appearing together, both parties demonstrate their commitment and confirm their identities. **Step 3: Complete the Application Form** The application form will ask for basic personal information such as full name, date of birth, address, and occupation. You will also need to provide your social security numbers. Be sure to answer all questions accurately and completely. Any misrepresentations or false statements could lead to the invalidation of the marriage license. Completing the application honestly ensures that your marriage license will be valid and allows officials to maintain accurate records. **Step 4: Pay the Fee** The fee for a marriage license varies by county but typically ranges from $35 to $100. Some counties accept cash only, while others may take credit or debit cards. Check with the specific county clerk's office for accepted payment methods. Paying the required fee supports the operations of the county clerks' offices and contributes to maintaining proper record keeping. **Step 5: Wait Three Days** Tennessee law requires a three-day waiting period between issuing the license and the actual wedding ceremony. However, if both applicants complete a premarital course taught by a certified provider, the waiting period can be waived. Observing this mandatory wait period guarantees sufficient time for reflection upon the decision to marry and reduces impulsive actions. **Step 6: Get Married!** Once the waiting period has passed, you have 30 days to perform the ceremony before the license expires. Afterward, the officiant, witness(es), and sometimes the newlyweds themselves, must sign the license. Then, within three days of the ceremony, the officiant must return the signed license to the county clerk's office. Getting married after fulfilling all requirements legally binds your union according to Tennessee laws. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Both parties must be present when submitting the application. * A valid photo ID and proof of age are essential. * Provide correct and up-to-date information on the application form. * Fees vary by county; bring appropriate payment method. * Observe the three-day waiting period unless you have taken a premarital course. * Perform the ceremony within 30 days of receiving the license. * Return the signed license to the county clerk's office promptly after the ceremony. [Chi on one side of coffee table, MOT on couch, SIS watching] Learn how to make a heart origami folding paper-craft using a dollar bill. This heart in particular is sometimes called "change of heart." Tags:How to Make an Origami Heart using a Dollar,change of heart,dollar heart,dollar origami,money origami,origami,origami heart Grab video code: Here, I'll show you how to make an origami heart out of a dollar. There are many different patterns out there and this one is sometimes called change of heart. You'll need a dollar bill or any U.S. paper currency. For this video however I'm going to be using a piece of paper approximately twice the size of a dollar so you can see what's going on more clearly. I want the pink side of this paper to show so I'm going to start by flipping it over. Fold the corner of one end down to form a triangle. Crease and then unfold. Now, fold the other corner down to form a triangle, crease and then unfold. Now, we'll pinch in the sides and squash it down to form a triangle, just like the water bomb base. And you'll have to repeat the process on the other side. If you have trouble pinching in the sides to form the water bomb base, you can flip this over. Fold the sheet back on its self to make a guideline. Now, when you flip back to front you can pinch the sides in a little bit easier. Now we're going to take this flaps and fold them up and in towards the middle. We're going to need to squash these flaps down to form a square. I like to use something with a pointed end to help. Now, we'll take the corners of each of the small squares and fold them in. When you do this, it kind of looks like a kite and now we're going to squash these triangles down. Again, I like to use something pointed to help. You'll need to repeat this process on the three other square parts. Now, we're going to fold these corners the other way. And here, we're going to get rid of the white space in the middle. To do this, fold it in half. And then fold one side down. Take one of the pointed ends and pull it backwards down out of the way. And you're almost done. We need to hide this tab on the bottom. This tab is bigger when you use a real dollar. So let's see it with the real dollar. Now you can simply fold this tab back and hide it out of the way. Or you can be a little fancy and invert the tab. Now, they call this change of heart because once you're done you can stick a quarter inside the center. And there you go. a dollar origami heart. Let's talk about simplifying expressions, just like we often do when we want to make things easier to understand or work with. In math, one way we simplify expressions is by getting rid of any square roots in the denominator of a fraction. This process is called "rationalizing the denominator." Let's consider this expression: |a / (a a)|. At first glance, it seems complicated because of those square roots in the denominator. But don't worry - let's break it down step by step! Our goal here is to eliminate the square root in the denominator while keeping the value of the expression the same. To do this, we will multiply both the numerator and the denominator by something called the "conjugate" of the denominator. The conjugate is formed by changing the sign between the two terms inside the square root. So, the conjugate of our original denominator, (a a), is simply (a a). Now, let's multiply both the numerator and denominator by (a a): |a / (a a)| | (a a)/ (a a)| When we multiply the denominator by itself (i.e., (a a) (a a)), we actually remove the square root since anything multiplied by itself equals one: ( x)^2 = x. Therefore, our new denominator becomes just a. So now, our initial expression transforms into: |a / a| Since dividing a number by itself always results in 1, we finally arrive at: |a^(1/4)| This form shows us that the given expression is equal to the fourth root of a, which is definitely less intimidating than the original appearance! And there you go - through understanding and applying the concept of conjugates and rationalizing denominators, we were able to simplify a seemingly complex mathematical expression. Practice makes perfect, so keep trying these steps on different problems, and soon enough, it will become second nature! Creating an iTunes account without a credit card is a great way to access various services offered by Apple, such as the App Store, Apple Music, and more, even if you don't have or want to use a payment method. This guide will walk you through the process step-by-step, providing detailed explanations and helpful tips along the way. **Step 1: Open iTunes on your computer** Start by launching the iTunes application on your Windows or Mac computer. If you haven't installed iTunes yet, download and install it from the official Apple website before proceeding. **Step 2: Sign out of any existing accounts** If there are already iTunes accounts signed in on this device, sign them out by clicking the profile icon at the top right corner of the window and selecting 'Sign Out'. It's essential to start with a clean slate since we're creating a new account without linking it to a credit card. **Step 3: Find a free app or song** Search for a free app or song within the iTunes store that you can use to initiate the creation of your new account. Make sure it doesn't require entering billing information during installation. Click 'Get' next to the chosen item. **Step 4: Create a new Apple ID** When prompted to sign in, click 'Create New Apple ID'. Fill out all necessary details like name, email address (ensure it's one you have access to), date of birth, and country. Remember, using accurate personal information is crucial because it verifies your identity when needed. **Step 5: Set up security questions and password** Choose three security questions and provide answers for each. These should be memorable but hard for others to guess. Then, enter and confirm your preferred password. Keep in mind that strong passwords typically include uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and special characters. **Step 6: Agree to terms & conditions** Read through the terms and conditions carefully, checking boxes where required. Note that some regions may ask for additional verification methods, so familiarize yourself with these requirements based on your location. **Step 7: Enter fake payment info** At the payment stage, select 'None' as the payment type. For the Billing Address section, look up a random street address in the same country you chose earlier. You might need to find an actual address for accuracy; websites exist specifically to generate dummy addresses if necessary. **Step 8: Verify your email address** Check your email inbox for a message from Apple regarding your newly created account. Follow their instructions to verify your email address, which usually involves clicking a confirmation link they send you. **Step 9: Log into your new iTunes account** Once verified, log back into iTunes using your brand-new credentials. Download the previously selected free app or song again, making sure no further prompts for payment arise. By following these steps meticulously, you now possess an active iTunes account unlinked to any form of payment! Enjoy exploring everything available within the vast ecosystem of Apple services. Just remember that certain features, especially those involving purchases, could potentially become limited due to this setup. A middle-aged woman and an old man stood clinging together. And I put on my um no, no! Title: How to Curl Your Hair with a Curling Iron Curling your hair with a curling iron can be an excellent way to add body, volume, and texture to your locks. With patience, practice, and the right technique, you'll soon master this skill and enjoy beautiful curls that last all day. This tutorial will provide you with an in-depth guide on achieving perfect curls using a curling iron. **Things You Need:** 1. A curling iron (choose one appropriate for your hair length and type) 2. Heat protectant spray 3. Hair clips or sectioning clips 4. Comb or brush 5. Optional: hairspray or texturizing spray ### Step 1: Choose the Right Curling Iron Select a curling iron based on your hair length and type. For short hair, consider a 1-inch barrel; medium to long hair typically works well with a 1 -inch barrel. Ceramic or tourmaline irons distribute heat evenly and are gentler on the hair compared to metal barrels. Adjustable temperature settings allow better control over heat levels-fine or damaged hair requires lower temperatures (around 200 F-300 F), while thicker hair benefits from higher temperatures (up to 400 F). ### Step 2: Prepare Your Hair Wash and dry your hair before starting. Freshly washed hair may not hold curl as effectively as slightly dirty hair due to natural oils. However, avoid using conditioner on the sections of hair you plan to curl, as this can weigh down the strands and make them more difficult to work with. Spritz heat protectant spray throughout your hair, focusing especially on the ends. This product shields your hair from damage caused by high heat and helps maintain shine. Make sure to cover every part of your hair that will come into contact with the curling iron. ### Step 3: Brush or Comb Your Hair Before plugging in the curling iron, gently detangle your hair with a comb or brush. Working out any knots prevents breakage when applying heat later on. Divide your hair into manageable sections depending on its thickness: thinner hair should be divided into fewer sections, whereas thicker hair might require smaller segments. Secure these sections with hair clips or sectioning clips to keep them organized during the styling process. ### Step 4: Set Up Your Workspace What did you have on your hands? Ah [breathes in] it's going to be so yummy. But isn't there another name for it? It could also connect to the Game Boy Player to allow special features and play Game Boy, Game Boy Color, and Game Boy Advance games. Lily and Ben were playing with their toys in the living room. Lily had a pair of scissors and some paper. She liked to cut out shapes and glue them together. Ben had a box of crayons and a big book. He liked to color and draw. "Look, Lily, I made a car!" Ben said, showing his picture. "It has four wheels and a door and a window." "That's nice, Ben," Lily said, not looking up. "I made a flower. It has five petals and a stem and a leaf." "Can you count the petals?" Ben asked, curious. "Of course I can," Lily said, proud. "One, two, three, four, five. See?" "Wow, you're good at counting," Ben said, impressed. "Can you count the wheels on my car?" "Sure," Lily said, glancing at his picture. "One, two, three, four. There." "Can you count the scissors?" Ben asked, pointing at her tool. "That's easy," Lily said, smiling. "One." "Can you count the scissors now?" Ben asked, grabbing another pair of scissors from the drawer. Lily frowned. "That's not fair, Ben. You can't change the number of scissors." "Why not?" Ben asked, giggling. "It's fun." "No, it's not," Lily said, annoyed. "It's cheating. Counting is only fun when the number stays the same." "Fine," Ben said, putting the scissors back. "Let's count something else then. How about the books on the shelf?" Lily looked at the shelf. There were many books of different sizes and colors. She felt a bit nervous. "That's too hard, Ben. There are too many books to count." "No, it's not," Ben said, confident. "I can count them. Watch me." He started to point at each book and say a number. "One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, eleven, twelve, thirteen, fourteen, fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen, nineteen, twenty, twenty-one, twenty-two, twenty-three, twenty-four, twenty-five, twenty-six, twenty-seven, twenty-eight, twenty-nine, thirty, thirty-one, thirty-two, thirty-three, thirty-four, thirty-five, thirty-six, thirty-seven, thirty-eight, thirty-nine, forty, forty-one, forty-two, forty-three, forty-four, forty-five, forty-six, forty-seven, forty-eight, forty-nine, fifty!" He stopped and smiled. "There are fifty books on the shelf, Lily. I counted them all." Lily was amazed. She clapped her hands. "Wow, Ben, you're amazing. You can count so high. How did you learn to count so well?" Ben shrugged. "I don't know. I just like to count things. It's fun." Lily nodded. She wanted to learn to count like Ben. She looked around the room and saw something enormous. She had an idea. "Ben, can you count the sofa?" she asked, pointing at the big piece of furniture. Ben looked at the sofa. He scratched his head. He wasn't sure how to count it. "The sofa?" he repeated. "How do you count the sofa?" Lily smiled. She had found something that Ben couldn't count. She felt clever. "You can count the cushions," she said, "or the legs, or the buttons, or the stitches, or the hairs, or the dust, or the crumbs, or the coins, or the bugs, or the..." Ben interrupted her. He didn't like her idea. He thought it was silly. "Stop, Lily, stop," he said, shaking his head. "That's not counting. That's nonsense. Counting is only fun when the things are clear and simple. Not when they are enormous and messy." Lily pouted. She thought her idea was fun. She wanted to challenge Ben. "Fine," she said, crossing her arms. "Then let's count something else. How about the stars in the sky?" Ben looked out the window. It was dark outside. He saw many bright dots in the black. He felt a bit scared. He didn't want to count them. "The stars?" he whispered. "But there are too many stars to count, Lily. They never end. They go on and on and on." Lily smiled. She had found something that Ben couldn't count. She felt brave. "Come on, Ben," she said, pulling his hand. "Let's go outside and try. It will be fun." Ben hesitated. He didn't want to go outside. He wanted to stay inside and count things he knew. "You, ready to die for the quire," said Bowman reproachfully, "to stick up for the quire's enemy, William!" A circle is a set of points in a plane that are all the same distance from a fixed point. This fixed point is called the centre and the set of points form the circumference. A radius is a line segment joining the centre of the circle to a point on the circumference. A chord is a line segment joining two points on the circumference. A chord divides a circle into two segments, the larger being the major segment and the smaller the minor segment. A diameter is a chord that passes through the centre of a circle. An arc of a circle is a portion of its circumference. A sector is the region enclosed by two radii and an arc. Two radii divide a circle into two sectors: a major sector and a minor sector. A tangent line to a circle is a line that touches the circumference but does not cross it. The angle between a tangent line and the radius at the point of contact is 90 . They only wished they had thought of the special p word sooner. The first area of the brain to be affected by Alzheimer's is the "transentorhinal region" which is part of the "medial temporal lobe" located deep within the brain. Neurons start dying in this area first. It then spreads into the adjacent entorhinal cortex (EC) which acts as a central hub, for a widespread network that handles signals for memory and movement(like a main train station with train tracks going to different areas). There are some sounds and letters that exist in Welsh but not in English, such as the letters (and sounds) ch and ll. The first sound is pronounced like the Scottish "Loch Ness", and an example Welsh word that uses the 'ch' is "bach", which means "small." "Ll" is a voiceless 'l,' and is made by placing the tongue on the top of the top gum, and blowing. A Welsh word that uses the 'll' is "llan", which means "church" and appears a lot in place names, including one called "Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch". Both 'ch' and 'll' are single letters in the Welsh alphabet, along with 'dd,' 'ff,' 'ng,' 'ph,' 'rh,' and 'th.' An' he would throw the mangos down to the crocodile to eat. What distinguishes a scientific method of inquiry is a question known as 'the criterion'. It is an answer to the question: is there a way to tell whether a concept or theory is science, as opposed to some other kind of knowledge or belief? There have been many ideas as to how it should be expressed. Logical positivists thought a theory was scientific if it could be verified; but Karl Popper thought this was a mistake. He thought a theory was not scientific unless there was some way it might be refuted. On the other hand, Paul Feyerabend thought there was no criterion. For him, "anything goes", or whatever works, works. " A chick tries to strut like the rooster." Now I don't know it's not going on. And [child coughs] they both yelled McDonald's. Step 1: Determine the Purpose of Your Shed Before choosing an outdoor storage shed plan, you need to determine what the primary purpose of your shed will be. This could include storing gardening tools, lawn equipment, bikes, or outdoor toys. Knowing the intended use will help you select a plan that meets your specific needs in terms of size, design, and features. Step 2: Measure Your Available Space Measure the area where you plan to build the shed to ensure that you have enough space. Take into account any setback requirements imposed by local building codes or homeowners associations. Also, consider factors such as accessibility, sunlight exposure, and proximity to other structures when selecting a location for your shed. Step 3: Research Different Types of Sheds There are various types of sheds available, including gable, hip roof, saltbox, and lean-to styles. Each type has its own advantages and disadvantages in terms of aesthetics, durability, and cost. For example, gable sheds offer more headroom but may require additional bracing due to their sloping rooflines. Hip roof sheds provide excellent wind resistance but can be more expensive to construct than simpler designs. Step 4: Consider Material Options Outdoor storage sheds can be built from materials like wood, metal, plastic, or vinyl. Wood is a popular choice due to its natural appearance and versatility; however, it requires regular maintenance to prevent rot and decay. Metal sheds are durable and low-maintenance but may rust over time if not properly protected. Plastic and vinyl sheds are lightweight and easy to clean but lack the charm and character of wooden structures. Step 5: Select a Suitable Plan Once you've determined the purpose, measured your space, researched different types, and considered material options, it's time to choose a suitable shed plan. Look for plans that match your preferred style, size, and budget. Websites like Ana White, My Shed Plans, and The Family Handyman offer a wide variety of free and paid shed plans to suit every need. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Always check local building codes and obtain necessary permits before beginning construction. * Choose a level site with good drainage to prevent water damage. * Opt for pressure-treated lumber or other rot-resistant materials when building a wooden shed. * Use high-quality fasteners and connectors to ensure structural integrity. * Install proper ventilation to reduce moisture buildup and extend the life of your shed. * Add skids or a concrete foundation for added stability and longevity. * Personalize your shed with paint, trim, or decorative elements to enhance curb appeal. By following these steps and guidelines, you'll be well on your way to choosing a proper outdoor storage shed plan for your garden. With careful planning and execution, you'll enjoy a functional and attractive addition to your outdoor living space for years to come. It's not what you're post play with. [XXX is you wanna or you gonna?] Comets are large balls of ice, rock and dust, but you can think of them as large dirty snowballs that hurtle around the solar system. For most of the time they are invisible, but as they get closer to the Sun, the ice starts to heat up and turn into gas. The water vapour and dust released by this "boiling" process forms an enormous tail that stretches out behind the comet. In fact, Comets have two tails. The first is the trail of heavy debris (rock and dust) left along the path of the comet, and is known as the dust tail. The second is a stream of lighter material (ionized gas) that is blown away by solar rays and so always points away from the Sun. This is known as the ion tail. The ion tail is generally the brighter of the two and therefore the only one we see without a good telescope. In the past, some comets have passed through Earth's orbit and left a trail of dust and rock. When we travel through these trails of past-comets each year we tend to get meteor showers. " Title: How to Add a Mac to a Homegroup Introduction: A homegroup is a feature in Windows operating system that allows multiple computers connected to a single network to share files and printers with one another. While homegroups are primarily designed for PCs running Windows, you can also add a Mac computer to the mix by following these steps. This will enable your Mac to access shared files from Windows-based machines within the same network, making file sharing easier than ever before. Step 1: Check Network Settings Ensure both your Mac and Windows systems are connected to the same local network. You can check this by going to System Preferences > Network (on your Mac) or Control Panel > Network and Internet > Network and Sharing Center (on your Windows machine). Look for the name of the Wi-Fi network or Ethernet connection you're currently using; they should match across all devices. Key tip: If possible, hardwire your Mac into the router via an ethernet cable during setup for better stability and speed. Step 2: Enable File Sharing on Your Mac Go to System Preferences > Sharing (on your Mac), then tick the box next to 'File Sharing'. Now select which folders you want to make accessible over the network under 'Shared Folders', and adjust permissions as needed in the Users section below. Guideline: Only share what you need to reduce potential security risks. Step 3: Install SMB Client on Your Mac SMB (Server Message Block) protocol enables cross-platform communication between different operating systems like Windows and macOS. It comes preinstalled on most modern versions of macOS but if it isn't already installed, open Terminal app (found in Applications > Utilities) and type `smbclient -V` followed by Enter. If the response includes something about Samba, you have it installed. If not, install it using a package manager such as Homebrew by typing `brew install samba`. Step 4: Join the Same Workgroup Every device on a network belongs to a workgroup. By default, Windows uses a workgroup named WORKGROUP while macOS doesn't belong to any specific workgroup. To join the existing workgroup, go back to System Preferences > Sharing (on your Mac) and click on 'Options'. From there, choose the user account you wish to use when connecting to other computers, ensure 'Share files and folders using SMB' is selected, and change the 'Windows Filesharing' option to your current workgroup name (usually WORKGROUP). Step 5: Connecting to the Windows Computer On your Windows PC, locate the folder you want to share, right-click on it, select 'Give Access to' > 'Specific people...' > 'Everyone', then set the permission level to 'Read/Write'. Remember the path of this shared folder because you'll need it later. Now, head over to your Mac, launch Finder, press Command + K, enter smb://<IP_ADDRESS>/<SHARED_FOLDER_NAME>, replacing <IP_ADDRESS> with the IP address of your Windows PC and <SHARED_FOLDER_NAME> with the actual name of the shared folder. Click 'Connect As...' and provide the necessary credentials. Conclusion: You've successfully added your Mac to a homegroup! With this setup, you can now seamlessly transfer files between your Windows and macOS devices without needing third-party software or cloud storage services. Just remember to maintain good cybersecurity habits, such as regularly updating your OS, using strong passwords, and limiting the amount of data exposed through shared folders. The position of the hands shows the time. The face of the clock is a flat disk. It will often have the numbers one through twelve on the face to make it easier to read. Analog clocks commonly have two or three hands. If it has two, there is a large hand or "minute hand" and a smaller hand, the "hour hand". Clocks with three hands also have a "second hand". The kids were having a boat ride. Title: How to Calculate the Sum of Interior Angles of a Polygon Polygons are 2-dimensional geometric figures formed by straight lines. The sum of interior angles of any polygon can be calculated using a simple formula. This tutorial will guide you through the steps required to calculate the sum of interior angles of a polygon with precision and ease. ### Step 1: Identify the Type of Polygon A polygon is defined as a closed figure made up of three or more line segments called sides. These sides intersect at their endpoints forming vertices (corners). Based on the number of sides, polygons are classified into various types such as triangles, squares, pentagons, hexagons, etc. To start calculating the sum of interior angles, first identify what type of polygon you're dealing with. **Key Tip**: Memorize the names and numbers of sides for common polygons: triangle (3), quadrilateral / square / rectangle (4), pentagon (5), hexagon (6), heptagon (7), octagon (8), nonagon (9), decagon (10). It becomes easier from here because every time you add a side, just remember that 'deca' means ten, so 'hecto' adds seven (for heptagon) and then keeps going up by one for each new name. ### Step 2: Understand the Formula The formula for finding the sum of internal angles in a polygon is derived from geometry principles. For any n-sided polygon, the sum S of its interior angles can be given by: S = (n - 2) * 180 This formula works regardless of whether the polygon is regular (all sides and angles are equal) or irregular (not all sides and angles are equal). **Guideline**: Make sure to substitute correct value for 'n', which represents the number of sides of the polygon while applying this formula. ### Step 3: Apply the Formula Using the identified number of sides (n), multiply it by 180 degrees, then subtract two from the result. So if we have an n-sided polygon, our calculation would look like this: S = (n * 180) - 360 Let's consider some examples: - **Triangle (3 sides)** Apply the formula: S = (3*180)-360. Here, n=3. Therefore, S = 180 . A triangle always has internal angles adding up to 180 degrees. - **Pentagon (5 sides)** Apply the formula: S = (5*180)-360. Hence, S = 540 . - **Octagon (8 sides)** Apply the formula: S = (8*180)-360. Thus, S = 1080 . By following these straightforward steps, you should now be able to accurately calculate the sum of interior angles of any polygon! Remembering the formulas and practicing calculations regularly will make this process second nature over time. Happy studying! Don't move it or they'll fall over. Jill was so happy she could jump to the top of the tree with Jake's help. Bake a Chemistry Cake Next time you bake a cake, consider this. The cake dough is not really a cake, but when it's heated in the oven, a chemical reaction occurs and new bonds are formed. How does heat change things? When it comes to heat changing a chemical reaction, there are two types. One is "exothermic," a reaction that produces heat, and the other is "endothermic," a reaction that takes heat in. When you make a cake, you a producing an endothermic chemical reaction which changes batter to baked! A few things can happen when you bake a cake. Some chemical reactions to keep in mind while doing this tasty experiment are: - Heat helps baking powder produce tiny bubbles of gas, which makes the cake light and fluffy. - Heat causes protein from the egg to change and make the cake firm. - Oil keeps the heat from drying out the cake. What You Need: - small bowl - several sheets of aluminum foil - pie pan - cooking oil - measuring spoons - cup or mug - index card - science journal (optional) Ingredients for one cake: You'll need to measure and mix this set of ingredients four times to complete all four experiments-with the exceptions that are given below. - 6 tablespoons flour - 3 tablespoons sugar - 1 pinch of salt - 2 or 3 pinches of baking powder - 2 tablespoons milk - 2 tablespoons cooking oil - 1/4 teaspoon vanilla - butter knife| - 1/3 of an egg (Break egg into a cup; beat until mixed, then use approx. 1/3 of it. Save the rest for 2 of the other cakes.) What You Do: - Wrap several sheets of aluminum foil around the outside of the small bowl to form a mold. - Remove your foil "pan" and put it in the pie pan for support. - Ask a parent to help you coat the inside of the foil "pan" with the cooking oil, or cooking spray so the cake doesn't stick. - Have a parent pre-heat the oven to 350 degrees. - Mix all of the dry ingredients together. - Now, add the wet ingredients (as stated in the ingredient list, only use 1/3 of the egg; save the rest for use with the other cakes, below). - Stir the wet and dry ingredients until smooth and all the same color. - Pour batter into the "pan." - Bake in oven for 15 minutes. - After 15 minutes, remove the cake from the oven, set aside, and let cool for tasting later (yum). - Label the first cake #1 on an index card. Make sure to label each cake with its number to separate differences when all they are all baked. Next, continue on to make three more cakes, but do the following: - Leave the oil out of one. Label the cake "#2 NO OIL" - Leave the egg out of another. Label the cake "#3 NO EGG" - Leave the baking powder out of the third. Label the cake "#4 NO BAKING POWDER" - After baking, have your child cut each cake in half and look inside. - Do the cakes look different from each other? - Do they taste different from each other? - What did the chemical change and use of heat do to cakes # 1-4? - Discuss all of the reactions that occurred with cakes #1-4. Tell your child to write about, or draw pictures of what they observed in a science journal. The zebra said aloud, "You shall be my new friend." The painted friend nodded in agreement. The zebra and his painted friend painted in the grass together casting beautiful colors across the meadow. This is against the Yogyakarta Principles. This is changing, however. The United Kingdom passed the Gender Recognition Act of 2004. But I think perhaps he was just less able to follow this one. Dealing with a selfish mother can be challenging and emotionally draining. It is important to approach this situation with care, empathy, and assertiveness. This guide will provide you with steps to help you deal with a selfish mother while maintaining your own well-being. **Step 1: Understand Your Emotions** Before addressing the issue of a selfish mother, take some time to understand and process your emotions. You may feel angry, hurt, or frustrated due to her behavior. Recognizing these feelings will allow you to communicate effectively and maintain control over your reactions. *Key tip:* Keeping a journal of your thoughts and feelings might help you better understand them and find healthy ways to express them. **Step 2: Identify Specific Behaviors** To address the problem constructively, pinpoint specific behaviors that make you believe she's being selfish. Is she constantly seeking attention? Does she disregard your needs and wants? Being clear about what triggers your frustration will make communication more effective. *Guideline*: Focus on observable actions instead of labeling her as 'selfish.' For example, say "You often interrupt me when I speak" rather than "You are so selfish." **Step 3: Communicate Openly** Discuss your concerns with your mother calmly and openly. Use "I" statements to convey how her actions affect you without blaming or attacking her. Example conversation starters could include: * "I feel unheard when..." * "It hurts my feelings when..." * "I would appreciate it if..." This approach encourages understanding and reduces defensiveness. *Key tip:* Choose an appropriate time and place for this discussion where both of you can talk freely without interruptions or distractions. **Step 4: Set Boundaries** Setting boundaries is crucial when dealing with a selfish person. Let your mother know what behaviors are acceptable and unacceptable to you. Make sure to enforce these limits consistently. Examples of boundary setting phrases: * "I won't tolerate being interrupted during our conversations." * "My personal space should be respected." * "I need time for myself, so please don't call me multiple times a day." Remember, setting boundaries isn't about controlling others but defining how you want to be treated. **Step 5: Practice Active Listening** Listen carefully to your mother's perspective even if you disagree with it. Show empathy by acknowledging her feelings and experiences. When she finishes speaking, summarize her points to ensure mutual understanding. Active listening shows respect and builds trust between individuals, which can improve overall communication. **Step 6: Encourage Shared Responsibility** If your relationship involves shared responsibilities (e.g., household chores), promote equal participation. Suggest creating a chore chart or rotating tasks. If she resists, gently remind her of the importance of teamwork and fairness. Sharing responsibility fosters cooperation and minimizes opportunities for one person to dominate or act selfishly. **Step 7: Seek Professional Help** If despite your efforts, the situation does not improve or becomes too overwhelming, consider consulting a mental health professional. A therapist or counselor specializing in family dynamics can offer guidance, mediation, and coping strategies tailored to your unique circumstances. *Key tip:* Remember that therapy is confidential, non-judgmental, and designed to support your emotional well-being. By following these steps, you can learn to manage interactions with a selfish mother while protecting your self-esteem and promoting healthier relationships. How about bringing them both over here. Make a "When I Grow Up" Poster Adults love to ask children what they want to be when they grow up. As a third grader, your child may have more of an idea of the types of careers that she may be interested in pursuing. This activity helps her explore possible career paths for the future and gets her to practice her writing skills. She may change her mind several times when she gets older, but this activity is a fun way to get her thinking about the future! What You Need: - Poster board What You Do: - Sit with your child and have her brainstorm about what she wants to be when she grows up. - If she has trouble coming up with anything, ask her to list off her favorite hobbies. Does she like to draw? Perform science experiments? Write? Do math? Then suggest a career associated with each of those hobbies. - Have her make a list of all the careers she mentions. - Then, go through the list and have her pick one of the careers she wants to learn more about. - Go to the library or use the internet to help her research about the job. Encourage her to find out the training required for the job and how a person in that field spends their day at work. - If it's possible, find a person for her to meet who does the job she's interested in and help her come up with questions to ask them. - After she has a pretty good understanding of the job, have her make a poster. - First, have your child write a title such as, "When I Grow Up, I Want To Be..." followed by the career of her choosing. - Then, invite your child to draw a picture of herself "on the job." For example, if she wants to be a business executive, have her draw a picture of herself sitting at a desk in an office. - Underneath the picture, have her write how she would spend her day if she had that job. Encourage her to include details about where the job is, what types of tasks she would be performing, and how it would benefit other people. - Hang the poster up in the house where everyone can see it, and be sure to save the poster so your child can see it when she's older! The pagan holiday "Samhain", which the "All Saints" holy day replaced, was also known as the "Day of the Dead". Many wiccans and modern pagans celebrate the "Day of the Dead". This is a "happy" holiday (even though it celebrates death). It is the day that some believed the souls of dead people come back to Earth. Pope Gregory III originally designated Halloween on November 1 as a time to honor all saints. Soon, All Saints Day incorporated some of the traditions of Samhain. Seoul () is the biggest city of the Republic of Korea (South Korea), and its capital. Its official name is Seoul Special City ( "Seoul Teukbyeolsi"). It has a population of 10 million which is about 1/5 of the Korean population and 1/7 of Korea peninsula, and covers an area of 610 km that is only 0.6% of South Korea. It is the 6th most populated city in the world, and 7th most crowded. Seoul is the center of politics, economy, culture, transportation, and education for South Korea. [6.7 sec; S makes 3 'I want it' hoots, silence, rustles] In 1961, Pres. Kennedy said the U.S. would land a man on the Moon before 1970. Build your own Moon habitat. You'll be ready when we return there someday. Let's go to the Moon! No human has walked on the Moon since the Apollo 17 mission in December 1972. This time, though, the astronauts will stay much longer than the few days of the Apollo 17 mission. So now, NASA's Exploration Technology Development Program is working on everything that will be needed to make the Moon a place where a crew of astronauts can live for months. Explorers from Earth will have to build their own habitat, or home. Their home must protect them like no home on Earth would ever need to do. Why? There is no air on the Moon. And the temperature varies from 387 degrees Fahrenheit BELOW zero (-233 Celsius) at night to 253 degrees Fahrenheit ABOVE zero (123 Celsius) in the day. Tiny micro-meteoroids (space rocks) rain down on the Moon's surface. And no atmosphere means no protection from the Sun's harsh radiation. So a Moon habitat for humans will have to be very tough and very sturdy. It will have to be air tight, so the inside can be pumped up with breathable air without exploding or leaking. The habitat will have to be cooled during the Moon day and heated during the Moon night. It will need a water recycling system, a power generating system, and food storage and preparation facilities. The materials to build the Moon habitat should be lightweight, since they will have to be boosted out of Earth's gravitational field using rockets.The habitat will have to be sent to the Moon in pieces and assembled by the explorers once they arrive. So it should be easy to put together, since the Moon explorers will be working in space suits. As with any Moon habitat, it must have an airlock. The airlock is a small room between the door to the outside and the door to the inside. Both doors must close very tight and not leak any air. Before the astronaut opens the outside door, the inside door must be closed. The astronaut enters the airlock and closes the outside door. Then the airlock is pumped full of air. Only then can the astronaut open the inside door to enter the habitat. Leaving the habitat, the astronaut (in a spacesuit, of course), steps into the airlock with the outside door closed. The inside door is then closed and all the air pumped out of the airlock, making it a vacuum, just like the outside. The astronaut opens the outside door and steps outside. You can be a Moon explorer too. Practice by building your own Moon habitat. Pretend you are an astronaut working with your team mates on the Moon to build your new home. Materials you will need to build one Moon habitat: - 148 sheets of newspaper (use a paper with large pages-tabloid size is too small-and use the full square spread) - Masking or packaging tape - Stapler (heavy-duty, if available) - Bed sheet, colored tissue paper, or more newspapers for "walls" (optional) - White glue or glue stick (optional) Construct the Moon habitat: Use four sheets of newspaper to build each log. Lay the sheets out flat, one on top of the other. Set the pencil in the corner and roll across on the diagonal. Use the pencil to help you get started, and then remove the pencil. Roll evenly, but don't try to make the logs as thin as the pencil. When you get to the opposite corner of the paper, you'll have a tube or log. Tape the log shut. Staple three logs together to create a triangle. Repeat until you have five triangles. Staple the five triangles to each other at their bottom corners. Add connecting logs across the top. Staple the remaining five logs together at the center to make a star. Then raise the five connected triangles, or walls, off the floor and staple the ends together to form a five-sided (pentagonal) structure. It helps to have one person hold up the walls while another person staples. Now staple the free ends of the star to the junctions of the triangles on the top of the base, and the structure will stand by itself. Repeat this process until you have 37 logs. Then trim the ends a bit, making sure all the logs are the same length. They will be around 30 inches long. Now you will need a big, open space in which to construct the habitat. Construct the "airlock:" You should have 12 logs left. These will make the "airlock." Staple three logs together to make another triangle (red in above drawing). Use two more logs (green) to attach the base of this triangle to one of the base logs of the "habitat." This triangle becomes the airlock door. Using three more logs (blue), create a square by stapling them around the "door." The side logs will be a bit too long, so cut them off to make a square. Use two more logs (yellow) to make a triangle to support the airlock at the top. To do this, staple the ends to the two top corners of the square and staple the other ends together where the triangles meet on the habitat. Use the last two logs (purple) to stabilize the airlock as shown in the drawing. Fill in with solid walls (optional): You can give the structure "solid" walls by either carefully draping a sheet or two over it or by covering each section with tissue paper or newspaper. To do this, smear glue onto the logs and gently press pieces of colored tissue paper or newspaper onto the triangles. Don't forget to leave a door! You can rip or cut off the loose edges of the tissue paper. Don't forget the NASA logo! Print it, cut it out, and pin or glue it to the outside of your Moon habitat. Tom is a curious cat. Digital photography uses a digital camera. Sometimes it is called "digital imaging". Like other cameras a digital camera has a lens, aperture, and shutter. The picture the lens makes is recorded by a light-sensitive electronic sensor. A digital camera does not use photographic film to record a picture. Digital photographs are stored in storage devices such as SD cards. They can later be transferred to a computer. Paper prints can also be made from digital pictures. Digital cameras are also not expensive to use, as there is no film to buy. Mommy is going to make a stack. A servant came and took their empty sherry glasses. The motor areas control voluntary movements, especially fine movements performed by the hand. The right half of the motor area controls the left side of the body, and vice versa. Association areas produce a meaningful experience of the world, and supports abstract thinking and language. This enables us to interact effectively. Slovakia (Slovak: "Slovensko") (Official name The Slovak Republic, "Slovensk republika") is a country with no access to the ocean in Central Europe. It is bordered by Austria in the southwest, Hungary in the south, Ukraine in the east, Poland in the north and Czech Republic in the northwest. Its capital city is Bratislava, the only capital of the world that borders with other two countries. Other main cities are Ko ice, Pre ov, ilina, Bansk Bystrica, Tren n, Nitra and Trnava. Slovakia has been a member of the European Union since 2004 and its official currency is the Euro. As your child grows, there are new ways you can teach manners: Teach polite words early. Two-year-olds can learn to say "please" and "thank you." Encourage your child to use names. For example, "Mrs. Jones, may I . . . ?" This is a polite way to get someone's attention. Don't overdo. It's okay if your child forgets to say "please" or makes other mistakes once in a while. You'll both tire of manners if you require perfection. Be polite when you correct your child. This helps your child learn from mistakes rather than be embarrassed by them. Remember that it takes time for preschoolers to learn manners. By being polite, you'll set a good example. Copyright Parent Institute I'll pop it if that's the XXX. [Hap tries to catch the ball with MOT's help] [turns to page with train that has buttons for wheels] ASK A SCIENTIST Question: How can a whale be a mammal and not a fish? Since fish and whales both have similar streamlined shapes and live underwater one might assume that they are the same kind of animal. So to understand why this isn't true, let's first take a look at a fish. If you have never seen a fish up close, you might ask someone to take you to a local pet store where fish are easy to observe. The first thing that you may notice is that fish are covered with scales. Next you may notice the gills. Gills are used to take oxygen from the water. Since the gills contain many blood vessels the oxygen from the water goes directly into the fish's bloodstream. (What you see when you look at the fish is the cover over the gills, called the operculum. The blood filled gills underneath can be seen when the fish opens the operculum to breathe.) Look closely at the fins and tail and you will see that they are transparent and contain spines which give them support. If you were to take the temperature of the fish, you would find that it was the same as or slightly above the temperature of the water. Fish are not able to regulate their body temperature, in other words they are cold blooded animals. Most fish lay eggs, and provide either no or very limited care to their babies. Whales are not so easy to find! They belong to the order Cetacea, which also includes dolphins and porpoises. So if you've never seen a whale you might think about their more familiar relatives. Whales and dolphins do not have scales. They do have some (very little!) hair. And there are no openings on these animals where the gills of the fish are located. Instead, they breathe through blow holes located on the top of their heads (whales have either one or two, depending on the kind of whale). The whale must come up to the surface of the water and breathe in air through these blow holes (the spout of water that you see come from the whale occurs when the animal exhales). The air then goes to the lungs where oxygen is extracted. The fins on the sides of their bodies are actually flippers, which have bones very similar to those in our hands and arms. Whales use the energy from the food that they eat to generate heat and thus maintain a temperature higher than the surrounding environment. We call them warm blooded animals. They do not lay eggs, but instead give birth to one calf. This calf is nursed with milk for a year or longer. The mother whale protects its calf and teaches it how to survive. Whales are very intelligent animals. Ask a Scientist appears Thursdays. Questions are answered by faculty at Binghamton University. Teachers in the greater Binghamton area who wish to participate in the program are asked to write to Ask A Scientist, c/o Binghamton University, Office of Communications and Marketing, PO Box 6000, Binghamton, NY 13902-6000 or e-mail firstname.lastname@example.org. Every day, Sam would play near the river. One day, when Sam was at the river, he saw a big wave. It was so big and smooth! Sam loved it and wanted to see it again. Sam thought and thought and came up with an idea. He decided to try to balance on his two hooves and see the wave again. He hopped up and down, up and down and slowly built up his balance. Finally, after a few tries, the wave appeared from around the corner and Sam saw it! He was so excited! "Hooray! I found it!", he said with a loud, happy voice. His friends heard him and came running to join Sam and his big, smooth wave. They all had the best time jumping, playing and balancing together next to the wave! Being a cosmopolitan individual means being open-minded, cultured, well-traveled, and comfortable in various social settings around the world. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you become a more cosmopolitan person: ### Step 1: Embrace Diversity To be a cosmopolitan individual, start by embracing diversity in all its forms - cultural, racial, religious, socioeconomic, etc. Expose yourself to different cultures, customs, traditions, and ways of life. This can be done through reading books, watching documentaries or foreign films, attending multicultural events, or engaging in conversations with people from diverse backgrounds. By doing so, you will develop an appreciation for the richness and complexity of human experiences beyond your own. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Challenge any prejudices or stereotypes you may hold. * Avoid making assumptions about others based on their appearance or background. * Practice active listening when interacting with individuals from different cultures. ### Step 2: Improve Your Communication Skills When you talk to daddy on the phone later will you tell him you made him a pepper? Pakistan has four seasons: a cool, dry winter from December through February; a hot, dry spring from March through May; the summer rainy season, or southwest monsoon period, from June through September; and the retreating monsoon period of October and November. The beginning and length of these seasons vary somewhat according to location. Rainfall can change radically from year to year, and consecutive patterns of flooding and drought are also not uncommon. " We won't have paper there so now does that respect you of doing it? view a plan This multidisciplinary plan is called Music Basketball and deals with Note Values 3, 4, 5, 6 Title - Music Basketball for sports-minded students By - Jamie Subject - Math, Music Grade Level - 3-6 Objective: Students will be able to identify the values of quarter, eighth, half, dotted half, and whole notes, as well as rests. Materials: a small basketball hoop that will hook over a chalkboard or door, and a soft ball that will fit through the hoop, a "free-throw" line made of a strip of tape on the floor. Flashcards with rhythm math problems on them. I use construction paper, and I draw rhythms as math problems, for example: "quarter note + quarter note + half note + whole note =" and on the opposite side of the card write the answer: 8 beats. (One 1/4 note = 1 beat). Divide the class into 2 teams. Have one person from Team 1 step up to the free-throw line. Hold up a flashcard. They have to add up the notes and give the correct answer to the problem to earn a shot. If they give the correct answer, they get to shoot the ball. If they make the shot, they get 2 points for their team. Then, someone from Team 2 gets a turn. To make it more challenging, you can move the line closer or farther away, add 3-pointers, or play "1 on 1 , where one person from each team steps up, and whoever answers the problem correctly first gets to take the shot. I use this game on my 5th & 6th grade boys, whose first love is basketball. They want to play it everyday, and now they can name note values in no time!! He was an atheist republican and considered Napol on a hero. His mother was an extreme Catholic Royalist. As Hugo's father was an officer, the family moved frequently.
